States Reorganisation Act, 1956

Statement: The States Reorganisation Act, 1956, enacted on the recommendations of the Fazl Ali Commission, fundamentally redrew India's internal boundaries and established a new territorial framework based largely on linguistic considerations.

The most significant territorial restructuring in independent India occurred through the implementation of the States Reorganisation Act, 1956.

The Act represented the culmination of years of debate regarding linguistic identity, administrative efficiency, and national integration.

It was enacted on the basis of recommendations made by the States Reorganisation Commission (Fazl Ali Commission), which had examined the issue in great detail.

Before 1956, India's states were grouped into four constitutional categories known as Part A, Part B, Part C, and Part D States.

This classification had emerged from historical and administrative arrangements inherited from the British period and the integration of princely states.

Over time, the system became increasingly complex and unsuitable for the evolving needs of democratic governance.

The States Reorganisation Act completely abolished this old classification structure and replaced it with a more rational and uniform territorial arrangement.

With effect from 1 November 1956, India was reorganized into 14 States and 6 Union Territories.

The new boundaries were drawn primarily on linguistic considerations while also taking into account administrative convenience, economic viability, and national unity.

The Act strengthened federal administration by aligning state boundaries more closely with linguistic and cultural identities.

It also reduced regional tensions that had emerged due to demands for linguistic recognition.

Many scholars regard the Act as one of the most important milestones in the evolution of Indian federalism.

The reorganization created a more stable and manageable administrative structure, which later facilitated the creation of additional states through constitutional procedures.

Even today, the territorial framework established in 1956 remains the foundation upon which subsequent state reorganizations have been built.

Commencement Limit Only

Statement: Part II of the Constitution (Articles 5 to 11) deals only with the persons who became citizens of India at the commencement of the Constitution on 26 January 1950. It does not provide a permanent code governing future acquisition or loss of citizenship.

Citizenship is one of the most fundamental aspects of a sovereign state because it determines the legal relationship between an individual and the nation.

When the Constitution came into force on 26 January 1950, India had recently experienced Partition, mass migration, and the integration of diverse territories.

As a result, the Constituent Assembly needed to identify who would immediately qualify as citizens of the newly independent Republic.

To address this urgent transitional requirement, the Constitution incorporated Part II (Articles 5 to 11).

These provisions were designed primarily to determine citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution and not to create a permanent citizenship framework.

Articles 5 to 8 classified various categories of persons, including residents of India, migrants from Pakistan, return migrants, and persons of Indian origin residing abroad.

The objective was to resolve citizenship questions arising from the unique historical circumstances surrounding Independence and Partition.

Unlike many constitutions that contain detailed citizenship codes, the Indian Constitution intentionally adopted only a temporary constitutional arrangement.

The framers believed that future citizenship matters would require flexibility and periodic legislative adaptation in response to changing social and political realities.

Therefore, the Constitution did not prescribe comprehensive provisions regarding future acquisition, renunciation, termination, or deprivation of citizenship.

Instead, it left the detailed regulation of citizenship to Parliament through ordinary legislation.

This approach enabled the legal framework governing citizenship to evolve over time without requiring repeated constitutional amendments.

Consequently, Part II serves primarily as a historical and transitional chapter rather than a complete citizenship code.

The detailed and continuing framework governing citizenship today is contained in the Citizenship Act, 1955, enacted by Parliament under constitutional authority.

Constitutional LocationPart II (Articles 5–11)

UPSC Revision: Part II (Articles 5–11) → Citizenship at Commencement (26 Jan 1950) Only → Transitional Provisions → No Permanent Citizenship Code → Detailed Framework Later Provided by the Citizenship Act, 1955.

Article 11 – Parliamentary Supremacy in Citizenship

Statement: Article 11 empowers Parliament to regulate all matters relating to citizenship, including acquisition, termination, deprivation, and naturalization. This provision grants Parliament comprehensive legislative authority over citizenship matters.

The framers of the Constitution recognized that citizenship is a dynamic concept that evolves with changing demographic, political, and international circumstances.

Instead of embedding an inflexible citizenship code within the Constitution itself, they chose to vest extensive authority in Parliament to regulate citizenship through legislation.

This objective was achieved through Article 11, one of the most important provisions in Part II of the Constitution.

Article 11 explicitly authorizes Parliament to make laws concerning the acquisition and termination of citizenship and all other matters connected with citizenship.

The provision ensures that Parliament possesses continuing authority to adapt citizenship laws according to the changing needs of the nation.

As a result, the citizenship provisions contained in Articles 5 to 10 do not operate as permanent limitations on Parliament's legislative competence.

Parliament may supplement, modify, or replace citizenship rules through ordinary legislation whenever necessary.

This constitutional flexibility became particularly important in addressing issues such as migration, overseas Indians, dual nationality concerns, refugee movements, and national security considerations.

Exercising the powers conferred by Article 11, Parliament enacted the Citizenship Act, 1955, which became the principal legislation governing citizenship in India.

The Act provides detailed provisions regarding citizenship by birth, descent, registration, naturalization, and incorporation of territory.

It also lays down procedures for renunciation, termination, and deprivation of citizenship.

Over the decades, Parliament has amended the Citizenship Act multiple times to address emerging challenges and policy requirements.

The constitutional design therefore places citizenship primarily within the domain of parliamentary legislation rather than constitutional rigidity.

Article 11 thus serves as the bridge between the Constitution's transitional citizenship provisions and the evolving statutory framework governing citizenship today.

ProvisionSignificance
Article 11Parliament's Citizenship Power
ScopeAcquisition, Termination, Naturalization & Related Matters

ImportanceFlexible Citizenship Framework
UPSC Revision: Article 11 → Parliament Gets Absolute Power over Citizenship → Can Regulate Acquisition, Termination, Naturalization & Related Matters → Led to Enactment of the Citizenship Act, 1955.

Article 9 – Single Citizenship Principle

Statement: India follows the principle of Single Citizenship, under which every citizen owes allegiance only to India. According to Article 9, any Indian citizen who voluntarily acquires the citizenship of a foreign state ceases to be an Indian citizen.

One of the distinctive features of the Indian constitutional system is the adoption of Single Citizenship for the entire country.

Unlike certain federal nations where individuals possess both national and regional citizenship, India recognizes only one citizenship—that of the Union of India.

The framers deliberately adopted this model to strengthen national unity and promote a common sense of identity among citizens.

This approach reflects the unitary spirit embedded within India's quasi-federal constitutional structure.

As a result, citizens enjoy equal political and civil status throughout the territory of India irrespective of the state in which they reside.

The constitutional foundation of this principle is found in Article 9, which addresses the consequences of voluntarily acquiring foreign citizenship.

Article 9 declares that a person shall not be a citizen of India if they voluntarily acquire the citizenship of another country.

The underlying principle is that citizenship involves exclusive political allegiance and loyalty to a sovereign state.

Consequently, India does not recognize unrestricted dual citizenship in the conventional legal sense.

When an Indian citizen voluntarily acquires the nationality of a foreign country, Indian citizenship stands terminated in accordance with the provisions of the Citizenship Act, 1955.

This rule ensures clarity regarding political rights, constitutional obligations, and diplomatic protection.

The principle also prevents conflicts of allegiance that may arise when an individual simultaneously owes obligations to multiple sovereign states.

Although India introduced the Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) scheme, OCI status is not equivalent to dual citizenship and does not confer full political rights.

Thus, the constitutional framework continues to preserve the doctrine of single and exclusive Indian citizenship.

Article 5 – Citizenship by Domicile

Statement: Article 5 granted citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution to persons who were domiciled in India and satisfied certain conditions relating to birth, parentage, or residence.

At the time of Independence, India faced the enormous task of determining who would be recognized as citizens of the newly established Republic.

To address this issue, the Constitution laid down specific commencement-based citizenship provisions in Part II.

Article 5 constitutes the primary provision dealing with citizenship by domicile at the commencement of the Constitution on 26 January 1950.

The concept of domicile refers to a person's permanent home and the place where they intend to reside indefinitely.

Article 5 required that an individual must be domiciled in the territory of India in order to claim citizenship under this provision.

In addition to domicile, the person had to satisfy at least one of three constitutional conditions.

First, the individual could qualify if they were born in the territory of India.

Second, citizenship could be obtained if either of the person's parents had been born in India.

Third, a person could qualify by having been ordinarily resident in India for not less than five years immediately preceding the commencement of the Constitution.

These alternative conditions were designed to accommodate diverse categories of residents living in India at the time of Independence.

The provision reflected the framers' intention to establish an inclusive citizenship framework while maintaining a genuine connection between the individual and the Indian state.

Article 5 played a crucial role in providing legal certainty during the transition from colonial rule to constitutional governance.

It remains historically significant because it identified the largest category of persons who automatically became citizens on 26 January 1950.

Although Article 5 operates only in relation to commencement citizenship, it remains an important topic in constitutional law and UPSC examinations.

Article 6 – Migrants from Pakistan

Statement: Article 6 deals with persons who migrated from Pakistan to India and provides different citizenship conditions depending on whether migration occurred before or after 19 July 1948, the date on which the permit system was introduced.

The Partition of India in 1947 triggered one of the largest migrations in human history, with millions of people crossing newly created borders between India and Pakistan.

This unprecedented movement of population created complex citizenship questions that required special constitutional treatment.

To address these circumstances, the Constitution incorporated Article 6, which specifically governs migrants who moved from Pakistan to India.

The provision distinguishes between migrants based on a crucial historical cutoff date: 19 July 1948.

This date is significant because the Government introduced a formal permit system to regulate migration between the two countries.

For persons who migrated from Pakistan to India before 19 July 1948, the Constitution adopted a relatively liberal approach.

Such individuals became citizens of India automatically, provided they or their parents or grandparents had been born in undivided India and they had settled in India.

The framers recognized that these migrants had generally relocated during the immediate upheaval of Partition and therefore deserved prompt constitutional recognition.

A different procedure applied to persons who migrated after 19 July 1948.

Since migration was now regulated through the permit system, such individuals could not obtain citizenship automatically.

They were required to apply for registration as Indian citizens before a designated authority.

Additionally, they had to reside in India for at least six months immediately preceding the application.

The distinction reflected the government's effort to balance humanitarian considerations with administrative control over migration.

Article 6 thus played a critical role in resolving citizenship issues arising directly from the Partition and remains one of the most historically significant citizenship provisions in the Constitution.

Article 7 – Migrants to Pakistan Who Returned

Statement: Article 7 deals with persons who migrated from India to Pakistan after 1 March 1947 but later returned to India under a permit for resettlement. Such persons could obtain citizenship only after fulfilling specified registration requirements.

The Partition of India resulted not only in migration from Pakistan to India but also in the movement of large numbers of people from India to Pakistan.

Many individuals who initially migrated to Pakistan later decided to return to India due to personal, economic, social, or political reasons.

This created a distinct constitutional issue that differed from the situation covered under Article 6.

To address these return migrants, the Constitution incorporated Article 7.

The provision applies to persons who migrated from India to Pakistan after 1 March 1947, a date chosen because it marked the beginning of large-scale migration associated with Partition.

As a general rule, such persons were not automatically entitled to Indian citizenship because they had chosen to migrate to Pakistan.

However, the Constitution recognized that some individuals subsequently returned to India intending to settle permanently.

Therefore, an exception was created for those who re-entered India under a valid permit for resettlement or permanent return.

Possession of such a permit demonstrated the government's approval of their return and intention to reside in India.

Even after returning, these individuals did not receive citizenship automatically.

They were required to reside in India for at least six months and thereafter apply for registration as citizens.

The registration process ensured that their eligibility and intention to settle permanently could be formally verified.

Article 7 thus balanced national interests with humanitarian concerns arising from the extraordinary circumstances of Partition.

It remains an important constitutional provision because it dealt with one of the most complex categories of migrants created by the division of British India.

Article 8 – Persons of Indian Origin Residing Abroad

Statement: Article 8 provides a citizenship pathway for persons of Indian origin residing outside India whose parents or grandparents were born in undivided India, subject to registration with an Indian diplomatic or consular representative.

At the time of Independence, a significant number of persons of Indian origin were living in various parts of the world due to migration, trade, employment, and colonial-era movements.

Many of these individuals maintained strong cultural and familial connections with India despite residing outside its territorial boundaries.

The framers of the Constitution considered it important to provide a constitutional mechanism through which such persons could claim Indian citizenship.

Accordingly, Article 8 was incorporated into Part II of the Constitution.

The provision applies to persons who are ordinarily residing outside India but possess ancestral links with the territory of undivided India.

To qualify, the individual, or either of their parents or grandparents, must have been born in India as defined under the Government of India Act, 1935.

This broad definition enabled many members of the Indian diaspora to establish eligibility through family lineage.

However, citizenship was not granted automatically under Article 8.

The eligible person was required to apply for registration as an Indian citizen before the diplomatic or consular representative of India in the country where they were residing.

The registration requirement ensured that citizenship was granted only to those who voluntarily expressed their desire to maintain a legal connection with India.

Article 8 reflected the Constituent Assembly's recognition of the global Indian community and its enduring relationship with the homeland.

The provision also demonstrated that Indian citizenship could be based not only on territory and residence but also on descent and ancestral origin.

Historically, Article 8 played an important role in extending constitutional recognition to Indians living abroad during the formative years of the Republic.

Although modern citizenship matters are governed by the Citizenship Act, 1955, Article 8 remains a significant constitutional provision regarding the overseas Indian community.

Citizenship by Birth (Jus Soli)

Statement: Citizenship by birth in India has been progressively restricted through amendments. For persons born in India on or after 3 December 2004, citizenship is granted only if both parents are citizens, or one parent is a citizen and the other is not an illegal migrant.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 recognizes several modes through which Indian citizenship may be acquired, one of the most important being citizenship by birth.

This principle is traditionally associated with the doctrine of Jus Soli, meaning “right of the soil,” under which citizenship is determined primarily by place of birth.

In its original form, Indian citizenship law largely followed an inclusive Jus Soli approach.

Any person born in India was generally entitled to Indian citizenship regardless of the nationality of their parents.

However, concerns relating to illegal migration, demographic changes, and border management gradually prompted legislative reforms.

As a result, Parliament introduced successive amendments that narrowed the scope of automatic citizenship by birth.

The most significant restriction emerged through amendments that culminated in the rules applicable from 3 December 2004.

Under the current framework, a person born in India on or after this date is not automatically entitled to citizenship merely because of birth within Indian territory.

Instead, additional parental qualifications must be satisfied.

A child becomes an Indian citizen if both parents are citizens of India at the time of birth.

Alternatively, citizenship is available if one parent is an Indian citizen and the other parent is not an illegal migrant.

If one parent is an illegal migrant, the child cannot claim Indian citizenship solely by virtue of birth in India.

This modification reflects a shift from a pure Jus Soli model toward a mixed approach that incorporates elements of parentage and legal status.

Consequently, citizenship by birth remains available, but subject to stricter statutory safeguards designed to regulate unlawful migration.

PeriodCitizenship Rule
Original PositionBroad Jus Soli Principle
After 3 Dec 2004Parental Conditions Mandatory
Condition 1Both Parents Citizens
Condition 2One Parent Citizen & Other Not an Illegal Migrant
Underlying ConcernIllegal Migration
UPSC Revision: Citizenship by Birth (Jus Soli) → Progressively Restricted → Born on or after 3 Dec 2004 → Both Parents Citizens OR One Parent Citizen + Other Not an Illegal Migrant → Automatic Birth Citizenship Curtailed.

Citizenship by Descent (Jus Sanguinis)

Statement: A person born outside India on or after 3 December 2004 acquires Indian citizenship by descent only if the birth is registered at an Indian consulate within one year and accompanied by a declaration that the child does not hold a foreign passport.

Citizenship by descent is based on the doctrine of Jus Sanguinis, meaning “right of blood,” where citizenship is derived from the nationality of one's parents rather than the place of birth.

This principle is particularly important for children born outside India to Indian citizens residing abroad.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 recognizes citizenship by descent as one of the principal modes of acquiring Indian citizenship.

Historically, the law allowed Indian citizenship to pass relatively easily from parents to children born overseas.

However, with increasing global mobility and concerns regarding multiple nationalities, Parliament introduced stricter procedural requirements.

Under the present framework, special rules apply to persons born outside India on or after 3 December 2004.

Such a person does not automatically become an Indian citizen merely because one or both parents are Indian citizens.

Instead, the birth must be formally registered at an Indian consulate located in the country of birth or residence.

This registration must ordinarily be completed within one year from the date of birth.

The registration requirement enables Indian authorities to maintain accurate records of citizens residing abroad and verify citizenship claims.

In addition, the parents must submit a declaration confirming that the child does not possess a foreign passport.

This condition reflects India's policy against unrestricted dual citizenship and seeks to ensure clarity regarding nationality status.

Failure to comply with these requirements may prevent the child from acquiring Indian citizenship by descent.

Thus, while the law continues to recognize citizenship through parentage, it subjects such citizenship to specific statutory procedures and safeguards.

Citizenship by Registration and Naturalization

Statement: The Citizenship Act, 1955 provides two important statutory routes to citizenship—Registration and Naturalization. Registration mainly benefits Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs) and spouses of Indian citizens, while Naturalization is available to foreign nationals who satisfy long-term residence requirements.

In addition to citizenship by birth and descent, the Citizenship Act, 1955 provides mechanisms through which individuals can become Indian citizens based on residence, ancestry, or association with India.

Two such methods are citizenship by registration and citizenship by naturalization.

Although both ultimately confer Indian citizenship, they apply to different categories of persons and involve distinct eligibility conditions.

Citizenship by Registration is generally intended for persons who already possess a close connection with India.

This category includes Persons of Indian Origin (PIOs), overseas Indians, and certain spouses of Indian citizens.

One of the most important conditions for registration is that the applicant must have been ordinarily resident in India for a specified period.

For spouses of Indian citizens, the law generally requires seven years of ordinary residence before registration can be granted.

The registration route therefore serves as a relatively simplified pathway for persons who have strong historical, familial, or cultural links with India.

Citizenship by Naturalization, on the other hand, is designed primarily for foreign nationals who do not possess such ancestral or familial ties.

Naturalization is granted only after the applicant demonstrates substantial integration into Indian society.

Under the prevailing legal framework, the applicant must have resided in India for an aggregate period of eleven years during the preceding fourteen years.

Additionally, the person must have resided continuously in India during the twelve months immediately preceding the application.

These requirements ensure that citizenship is granted only after a prolonged and genuine association with the country.

Together, registration and naturalization provide flexible legal mechanisms for integrating eligible individuals into the Indian citizenship framework while safeguarding national interests.

ModeKey BeneficiariesResidence Requirement

Citizenship by Incorporation of Territory

Statement: When a foreign territory becomes part of India, the Central Government has the authority to specify which persons belonging to that territory shall become citizens of India. This mode of acquisition is known as Citizenship by Incorporation of Territory.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 recognizes that territorial boundaries of a nation may change due to political agreements, treaties, mergers, or other constitutional developments.

In such circumstances, questions arise regarding the citizenship status of the inhabitants of the newly acquired territory.

To address this situation, the Act provides a special mode of acquiring citizenship known as Citizenship by Incorporation of Territory.

Under this provision, when any foreign territory becomes part of India, citizenship is not granted automatically to every resident by constitutional mandate.

Instead, the law empowers the Central Government to determine and notify the categories of persons who shall be recognized as Indian citizens.

This approach provides administrative flexibility and allows the government to frame citizenship arrangements suited to the specific circumstances of territorial integration.

The provision has been invoked on several occasions in India's post-independence history when territories were integrated into the Union.

A notable example is the incorporation of the former French settlement of Pondicherry (now Puducherry) into India.

Following its merger, the Government issued the Citizenship Order, 1962 specifying the persons who would acquire Indian citizenship.

Similar principles have guided citizenship arrangements relating to other territories that became part of India through constitutional and diplomatic processes.

The rationale behind this provision is that territorial integration often involves unique legal, historical, and demographic considerations that cannot be addressed through a uniform rule.

By empowering the Central Government to issue specific orders, the law ensures a smooth transition of inhabitants into the Indian citizenship framework.

Thus, citizenship by incorporation of territory represents a specialized but constitutionally significant mode of citizenship acquisition under the Citizenship Act, 1955.

Renunciation of Citizenship

Statement: Renunciation is the voluntary surrender of Indian citizenship by an adult citizen. When a person renounces Indian citizenship, every minor child of that person also loses Indian citizenship, although such children may resume it within one year of attaining majority.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 provides mechanisms through which Indian citizenship may be voluntarily or involuntarily lost.

The first and most straightforward method is Renunciation of Citizenship.

Renunciation occurs when an adult Indian citizen voluntarily declares their intention to give up Indian citizenship.

This process is based on the principle that citizenship is a legal relationship that may be terminated by the free choice of the individual.

A declaration of renunciation must be made in the manner prescribed by law and accepted by the competent authority.

Once the declaration is registered, the individual ceases to be an Indian citizen from the date prescribed under the Act.

An important feature of renunciation is its effect on minor children.

The law provides that when a person validly renounces Indian citizenship, every minor child of that person also loses Indian citizenship automatically.

This rule is intended to maintain consistency in the citizenship status of parents and dependent children.

However, recognizing that minors cannot independently determine their nationality, the Act grants them a special safeguard.

When such a child attains the age of eighteen years, they are given an opportunity to reconsider their citizenship status.

The child may apply to resume Indian citizenship within one year of attaining majority.

This provision balances parental decisions with the future autonomy of the child.

Renunciation therefore represents a voluntary and legally regulated pathway through which citizenship may be surrendered while preserving limited protections for affected minors.

FeatureProvision
Mode of LossRenunciation
NatureVoluntary
Eligible PersonAdult Citizen
Effect on Minor ChildrenAutomatically Lose Citizenship

UPSC Revision: Renunciation → Voluntary Surrender by Adult Citizen → Minor Children Also Lose Citizenship → Child Can Resume Citizenship Within 1 Year After Attaining Majority.

Termination of Citizenship

Statement: Termination occurs automatically when an Indian citizen voluntarily acquires the citizenship of another country. However, this provision does not operate during a war in which India is engaged.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 distinguishes between voluntary surrender of citizenship and automatic loss resulting from the acquisition of foreign nationality.

The latter mechanism is known as Termination of Citizenship.

Termination reflects India's long-standing commitment to the principle of Single Citizenship, under which an individual is expected to owe exclusive allegiance to the Indian State.

Unlike renunciation, termination does not require a formal declaration surrendering Indian citizenship.

Instead, the loss of citizenship occurs automatically by operation of law when a citizen voluntarily acquires the citizenship of another sovereign state.

The rationale behind this rule is that acquisition of foreign citizenship signifies acceptance of political allegiance to another country.

Consequently, the person can no longer simultaneously retain Indian citizenship under the ordinary legal framework.

The moment foreign citizenship is voluntarily acquired, Indian citizenship stands terminated in accordance with the provisions of the Citizenship Act.

This process is therefore automatic and does not depend upon a separate order of the Government.

The provision is closely linked with Article 9 of the Constitution, which embodies the principle that voluntary acquisition of foreign nationality is incompatible with continued Indian citizenship.

However, the Act contains an important exception designed to protect national interests during extraordinary circumstances.

If India is engaged in a war, the termination provision does not operate automatically.

This safeguard prevents complications that could arise from questions of nationality and allegiance during periods of armed conflict.

Termination is thus a statutory mechanism that preserves the integrity of India's single citizenship model while incorporating limited protection during wartime conditions.

FeatureProvision
Mode of LossTermination
NatureAutomatic
TriggerVoluntary Acquisition of Foreign Citizenship
Constitutional LinkArticle 9
ExceptionDoes Not Apply During War
UPSC Revision: Termination → Automatic Loss of Indian Citizenship → Triggered by Voluntary Acquisition of Foreign Citizenship → Based on Article 9 & Single Citizenship Principle → Not Applicable During War.

Deprivation of Citizenship

Statement: Deprivation is the compulsory termination of citizenship by the Central Government on specified grounds such as fraud, disloyalty, unlawful dealings with an enemy during war, or certain criminal convictions after naturalization.

The Citizenship Act, 1955 recognizes that citizenship may sometimes be obtained or retained in circumstances that are contrary to national interests.

To address such situations, the Act empowers the Central Government to compulsorily remove citizenship through a process known as Deprivation of Citizenship.

Unlike renunciation, which is voluntary, and termination, which operates automatically by law, deprivation is an executive action initiated by the Government.

This power is exercised only on specific grounds expressly provided under the Act.

One important ground is the acquisition of citizenship through fraud, false representation, or concealment of material facts.

If citizenship was obtained by misleading the authorities, the Government may deprive the person of that citizenship.

Another ground is disloyalty to the Constitution of India or conduct demonstrating lack of allegiance to the nation.

The law also permits deprivation where a citizen has unlawfully traded or communicated with an enemy during a war involving India.

Such conduct is considered incompatible with the obligations of citizenship and national security.

An additional ground applies particularly to persons who acquired citizenship through naturalization.

If, within five years of obtaining citizenship by naturalization, the person is sentenced to imprisonment in any country for a period of two years or more, the Government may initiate deprivation proceedings.

The objective of this provision is to ensure that citizenship granted through naturalization is enjoyed only by individuals who maintain good conduct and loyalty.

Before deprivation is finalized, the affected individual is generally afforded procedural safeguards and an opportunity to present their case.

Deprivation therefore serves as a protective mechanism that enables the State to safeguard the integrity of Indian citizenship and national interests.

FraudCitizenship Obtained by False Representation or Concealment

Special Provisions for Assam – Section 6A

Statement: Following the Assam Accord of 1985, Section 6A was inserted into the Citizenship Act, 1955 to create special citizenship provisions for Assam and establish a unique framework for dealing with migrants from Bangladesh.

The state of Assam experienced significant demographic changes during the decades following Independence due to migration from the territory that later became Bangladesh.

Concerns regarding illegal migration, cultural identity, electoral representation, and resource distribution gradually intensified across the state.

These concerns culminated in the large-scale Assam Movement (1979–1985), led primarily by student organizations demanding the identification and removal of illegal migrants.

After prolonged negotiations between the Government of India and representatives of the movement, the historic Assam Accord was signed on 15 August 1985.

The Accord sought to balance humanitarian considerations with the protection of Assam's linguistic, cultural, and political interests.

To implement the citizenship-related commitments of the Accord, Parliament inserted Section 6A into the Citizenship Act, 1955.

This provision created a special legal regime applicable exclusively to the state of Assam.

Unlike the general citizenship provisions applicable elsewhere in India, Section 6A established distinct rules for determining the citizenship status of persons who had migrated from Bangladesh into Assam.

The provision acknowledged the unique historical circumstances of Assam and the migration patterns associated with the period surrounding the creation of Bangladesh in 1971.

As a result, Assam became the only state to receive a separate statutory citizenship framework within the Citizenship Act.

Section 6A remains one of the most debated and significant citizenship provisions in modern India because it directly links citizenship determination with historical migration and regional identity.

Its implementation continues to influence discussions relating to migration, the National Register of Citizens (NRC), and constitutional governance in Assam.

FeatureDetails
Legal ProvisionSection 6A, Citizenship Act, 1955
OriginAssam Accord, 1985
ApplicabilityOnly Assam
PurposeRegulate Citizenship of Migrants from Bangladesh
SignificanceSpecial Citizenship Regime for Assam
UPSC Revision: Assam Movement → Assam Accord (1985) → Section 6A Inserted into Citizenship Act → Special Citizenship Provisions for Assam → Deals with Migrants from Bangladesh.

The Dual Cut-Off Dates under Section 6A

Statement: Section 6A of the Citizenship Act established two separate cut-off dates for migrants from Bangladesh entering Assam. Those who entered before 1 January 1966 received citizenship immediately, while those who entered between 1 January 1966 and 25 March 1971 underwent a special process before being regularized.

The most distinctive feature of Section 6A is the creation of a dual cut-off system for determining the citizenship status of migrants who entered Assam from Bangladesh.

This framework was designed as a compromise between humanitarian concerns and the demand to protect the demographic and political interests of Assam.

The first category covers persons who entered Assam from the territory of present-day Bangladesh before 1 January 1966.

Such individuals were granted Indian citizenship without any special waiting period or restrictions.

The law treated them as ordinary citizens from the date specified under the statutory framework.

The second category applies to persons who entered Assam between 1 January 1966 and 25 March 1971.

The date of 25 March 1971 was chosen because it immediately preceded the outbreak of the Bangladesh Liberation War, which triggered massive cross-border migration.

Unlike the earlier group, these migrants were initially identified and recorded as foreigners under the prescribed legal process.

Although they were permitted to remain in India, they did not immediately enjoy full political rights.

Their names were removed from electoral rolls, and their voting rights remained suspended for a period of ten years.

After the completion of this ten-year period, they were regularized and granted full citizenship rights, including the right to vote.

The dual cut-off mechanism therefore created a distinction between earlier migrants and those who entered during the politically sensitive years preceding Bangladesh's independence.

This framework remains a central component of Assam's special citizenship regime and continues to be important for constitutional and citizenship-related debates.

Migration PeriodCitizenship Consequence
Before 1 January 1966Immediate Citizenship
1 January 1966 – 25 March 1971Detected as Foreigners Initially
Voting RightsSuspended for 10 Years
After 10 YearsRegularized as Citizens
Key Cut-Off Date25 March 1971
UPSC Revision: Section 6A → Before 1 Jan 1966 = Immediate Citizenship → 1 Jan 1966 to 25 Mar 1971 = Foreigner Status + 10-Year Voting Ban → Thereafter Regularized as Citizens.

CAA 2019 – Accelerated Naturalization Framework

Statement: The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 (CAA) amended the Citizenship Act, 1955 by providing a fast-tracked citizenship pathway for specified religious minorities from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan, reducing the residency requirement from 11 years to 5 years.

The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019 represents one of the most significant amendments made to the Citizenship Act, 1955 in recent decades.

The amendment was enacted with the objective of providing relief to certain religious minority communities who had migrated to India after facing religious persecution or fear of persecution in neighboring countries.

Prior to the amendment, such migrants generally had to satisfy the standard naturalization requirement of eleven years of residence before becoming eligible for Indian citizenship.

The CAA created a special exception for specific categories of migrants.

Under the amended framework, eligibility was extended to members of six religious communities: Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians.

The amendment applies only to persons originating from three neighboring countries—Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan.

These countries were identified because they have Islam as the state religion or a dominant religious framework, and the amendment sought to address the concerns of specified minority groups residing there.

To qualify, the migrant must have entered India on or before 31 December 2014.

The amendment also exempts eligible persons from being treated as illegal migrants for the purposes of citizenship law.

Its most important legal consequence is the reduction of the residency requirement for naturalization.

Instead of the ordinary requirement of eleven years, eligible applicants may seek citizenship after completing only five years of residence.

This accelerated naturalization process constitutes the central feature of the CAA framework.

The amendment therefore created a distinct statutory route to Indian citizenship for specified migrant communities while retaining the general naturalization rules for all other applicants.

LawCitizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019
Eligible CommunitiesHindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, Christians
