UPSC Revision: Fundamental Duties → Recommended by Swaran Singh Committee (1976) → Borrowed from USSR Constitution → Introduced during Emergency to promote responsible citizenship.

42nd Amendment Act, 1976 – Incorporation of Fundamental Duties

Statement: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 inserted Part IV-A and Article 51A into the Constitution, introducing a code of 10 Fundamental Duties for Indian citizens.

The recommendation of the Swaran Singh Committee led to a major constitutional change through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976.

This amendment inserted a new chapter known as Part IV-A into the Constitution.

Part IV-A consists of a single provision, Article 51A, which enumerates the Fundamental Duties of citizens.

At the time of its introduction, Article 51A contained a list of 10 Fundamental Duties applicable to every citizen of India.

These duties include respecting the Constitution, cherishing the ideals of the freedom struggle, protecting national unity, safeguarding public property, preserving cultural heritage, and protecting the natural environment.

The duties were intended to remind citizens that rights and responsibilities must coexist in a democratic society.

Interestingly, the Swaran Singh Committee had recommended only 8 duties, whereas Parliament ultimately incorporated 10 duties.

The committee had also suggested that Parliament should provide penalties for non-compliance with Fundamental Duties.

However, this recommendation was not accepted, and no direct constitutional penalties were created for violating these duties.

The inclusion of Fundamental Duties represented an effort to strengthen civic consciousness and encourage active participation in nation-building.

Thus, the 42nd Amendment gave constitutional recognition to citizens' obligations toward the nation alongside their constitutional rights.

86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002 – Addition of the 11th Fundamental Duty

Statement: The 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002 added the 11th Fundamental Duty, making it the responsibility of parents and guardians to provide educational opportunities to children between 6 and 14 years of age.

The Constitution originally contained ten Fundamental Duties under Article 51A.

To strengthen the national commitment toward universal education, Parliament enacted the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002.

This amendment is best known for introducing Article 21A, which made free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14 years.

In addition to creating this right, the amendment also introduced a corresponding responsibility for parents and guardians.

A new clause, Article 51A(k), was inserted into the list of Fundamental Duties.

This provision makes it the duty of every parent or guardian to provide opportunities for education to their child or ward between the ages of 6 and 14 years.

The amendment reflects the constitutional principle that rights and duties should operate together.

While the State is responsible for providing educational facilities, parents and guardians are expected to ensure that children avail themselves of those opportunities.

The addition of this duty increased the total number of Fundamental Duties from 10 to 11.

It also highlighted education as a national priority and an essential instrument for social and economic development.

Thus, the 86th Amendment established a constitutional partnership between the State and citizens in achieving universal elementary education.

UPSC Revision: 86th Amendment (2002) → Added Article 51A(k) → Parent/guardian must provide education opportunities to children aged 6–14 → Fundamental Duties increased from 10 to 11.

Fundamental Duties – Non-Justiciable Nature and Applicability

Statement: Fundamental Duties are non-justiciable obligations addressed exclusively to Indian citizens and do not carry direct constitutional penalties for their violation.

Fundamental Duties were introduced into the Constitution to remind citizens of their responsibilities toward the nation and society.

However, like the Directive Principles of State Policy, these duties are non-justiciable in nature.

This means that no court can compel a citizen to perform a Fundamental Duty, nor can a citizen be directly prosecuted under the Constitution for failing to perform one.

The Constitution does not provide any specific legal penalty for the violation of Fundamental Duties.

Unlike Fundamental Rights, which are enforceable through courts, Fundamental Duties primarily function as moral and civic obligations.

Another important feature is that Fundamental Duties are addressed exclusively to Indian citizens.

They do not apply to foreigners residing in India.

The framers of Part IV-A intended these duties to promote patriotism, civic consciousness, environmental responsibility, national integration, and respect for constitutional values.

Although they are not directly enforceable, courts often refer to Fundamental Duties while interpreting laws and constitutional provisions.

Parliament may also enact laws that indirectly enforce specific duties through statutory provisions.

Therefore, while Fundamental Duties lack direct judicial enforceability, they continue to play an important role in shaping responsible citizenship and constitutional culture.

NatureNon-Justiciable
Applicable ToCitizens Only
Applies to ForeignersNo
Constitutional PenaltyNone

UPSC Revision: Fundamental Duties → Non-justiciable → Apply only to citizens → No direct constitutional penalty → Intended to promote responsible citizenship.

Verma Committee (1999) and Statutory Enforcement of Fundamental Duties

Statement: The Verma Committee (1999) identified several existing laws that indirectly enforce Fundamental Duties, demonstrating that some duties have statutory backing despite being non-justiciable.

Although Fundamental Duties are non-justiciable and do not carry direct constitutional penalties, several duties are supported by ordinary legislation enacted by Parliament.

To examine this aspect, the government constituted the Justice J.S. Verma Committee in 1999.

The committee was tasked with identifying legal provisions that already existed for implementing and enforcing Fundamental Duties.

After reviewing various statutes, the committee concluded that many duties possess indirect legal support through existing laws.

One important example is the Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971, which penalizes disrespect shown to the National Flag, the Constitution, and the National Anthem.

This law supports the Fundamental Duty requiring citizens to respect national symbols and constitutional ideals.

The committee also highlighted the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972, which contributes to the duty of protecting the natural environment and safeguarding wildlife.

Similarly, the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 reinforces the constitutional duty relating to environmental protection and conservation of forests.

These examples demonstrate that while Fundamental Duties themselves are not directly enforceable through courts, Parliament can enact legislation that gives practical effect to them.

The Verma Committee therefore emphasized that many duties are already backed by a legal framework and are not merely symbolic declarations.

Its findings strengthened the understanding that Fundamental Duties can influence public behavior through statutory mechanisms even without direct constitutional sanctions.

UPSC Revision: Verma Committee (1999) → Identified statutory backing for Fundamental Duties → National Honour Act (1971), Wildlife Protection Act (1972), Forest Conservation Act (1980) → Duties indirectly enforceable through laws.

Introduction of Fundamental Duties through the 42nd Amendment

Statement: Fundamental Duties were not part of the original Constitution and were inserted through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 by introducing Part IV-A and Article 51A.

When the Constitution of India came into force on 26 January 1950, it contained Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles of State Policy but did not include a separate chapter on Fundamental Duties.

The framers believed that citizens would naturally discharge their civic responsibilities in a democratic society.

However, during the period of the National Emergency (1975–77), the government considered it necessary to explicitly incorporate citizens' obligations into the Constitution.

As a result, the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 inserted a new chapter known as Part IV-A.

This Part contains Article 51A, which enumerates the Fundamental Duties of citizens.

The idea was borrowed from the Constitution of the former USSR (Soviet Union), which emphasized duties alongside rights.

The inclusion of Fundamental Duties reflected the belief that citizens should not only enjoy constitutional rights but should also contribute to preserving national unity, integrity, and constitutional values.

These duties were intended to strengthen civic discipline, patriotism, and responsibility toward society and the nation.

Although they are not directly enforceable through courts, they serve as important moral and constitutional obligations.

The introduction of Part IV-A therefore completed the constitutional framework by emphasizing both rights and responsibilities.

Swaran Singh Committee Recommendations on Fundamental Duties

Statement: The Swaran Singh Committee recommended the inclusion of Fundamental Duties, but Parliament rejected several of its proposals, including penalties for non-compliance and a duty to pay taxes.

In 1976, during the period of National Emergency, the government constituted the Swaran Singh Committee to examine constitutional reforms and recommend measures to strengthen civic responsibility among citizens.

The committee concluded that while citizens enjoyed numerous Fundamental Rights, there should also be a clearly defined set of duties toward the nation.

Accordingly, it recommended the inclusion of eight Fundamental Duties in the Constitution.

The committee believed that constitutional recognition of duties would promote patriotism, discipline, national integration, and respect for constitutional institutions.

However, Parliament did not accept all of the committee's recommendations.

One major recommendation that was rejected was the proposal to impose penalties or punishments for non-compliance with Fundamental Duties.

The committee had suggested that citizens who deliberately violated these duties could face legal consequences.

Parliament chose not to incorporate this idea, thereby ensuring that Fundamental Duties remained non-justiciable and primarily moral obligations.

Another important recommendation that was rejected was the proposed duty to pay taxes.

Although the committee viewed tax payment as a civic responsibility, Parliament decided not to include it among the Fundamental Duties listed in Article 51A.

As a result, the final constitutional provisions differed significantly from the committee's original recommendations.

The Swaran Singh Committee nevertheless remains historically important because its recommendations formed the foundation for the eventual inclusion of Fundamental Duties in the Constitution.

The 10+1 Structure of Fundamental Duties

Statement: Fundamental Duties originally consisted of 10 duties under the 42nd Amendment, with the 11th duty being added later through the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002.

When Fundamental Duties were introduced through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976, Article 51A contained a list of 10 Fundamental Duties.

These duties were intended to promote patriotism, constitutional awareness, environmental responsibility, scientific temper, and national unity.

For more than two decades, the constitutional code of duties remained limited to these ten provisions.

A significant change occurred through the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002, which strengthened the constitutional commitment toward universal education.

In addition to introducing Article 21A (Right to Education), the amendment inserted a new duty under Article 51A(k).

This provision makes it the duty of every parent or guardian to provide opportunities for education to his or her child or ward.

The duty specifically applies to children between the ages of 6 and 14 years.

This addition reflected the belief that achieving universal elementary education requires cooperation between the State and citizens.

As a result of this amendment, the total number of Fundamental Duties increased from 10 to 11.

The 11th duty is unique because it directly connects a Fundamental Duty with a Fundamental Right, namely the Right to Education under Article 21A.

Today, Article 51A contains eleven duties that collectively emphasize civic responsibility, national commitment, and constitutional values.

Fundamental Duties Apply Exclusively to Indian Citizens

Statement: Unlike certain Fundamental Rights that are available to all persons, Fundamental Duties under Article 51A apply strictly and exclusively to Indian citizens.

The Constitution distinguishes between rights that belong to all persons and obligations that are specifically imposed upon citizens.

Fundamental Duties, contained in Part IV-A under Article 51A, are addressed exclusively to the citizens of India.

This means that only Indian citizens are expected to observe and perform these constitutional duties.

Foreign nationals residing, studying, or working in India are not legally bound by the provisions of Article 51A.

This feature contrasts with several Fundamental Rights, such as Articles 14, 20, and 21, which are available to all persons irrespective of citizenship.

The framers of the amendment believed that duties toward the Constitution, national symbols, sovereignty, culture, and public property should primarily rest upon those who are members of the Indian political community.

Therefore, the constitutional obligations enumerated in Article 51A are linked directly to citizenship status.

The exclusive applicability of Fundamental Duties also reflects the idea that citizenship carries both privileges and responsibilities.

While citizens enjoy political rights and constitutional protections, they are also expected to contribute toward national unity, integrity, and development.

Consequently, every duty listed in Article 51A—from respecting the Constitution to protecting the environment—is intended solely for Indian citizens.

This remains one of the key distinctions between Fundamental Rights and Fundamental Duties in the Indian constitutional framework.

UPSC Revision: Fundamental Duties → Article 51A → Apply only to Indian citizens → Not applicable to foreigners → Major distinction from several Fundamental Rights.

Non-Justiciable Nature of Fundamental Duties

Statement: Fundamental Duties are non-justiciable obligations. They cannot be directly enforced by courts, and the Constitution does not prescribe any punishment for their violation.

Fundamental Duties were incorporated into the Constitution to promote responsible citizenship and strengthen commitment to constitutional values.

However, unlike Fundamental Rights, they are not enforceable through judicial proceedings.

This characteristic is known as their non-justiciable nature.

A citizen cannot approach a court to compel another citizen to perform a Fundamental Duty, nor can a court directly punish a person solely for failing to discharge one of these duties.

The Constitution deliberately avoids creating a system of direct judicial enforcement for Article 51A.

Furthermore, no specific penalties or punishments are written into the constitutional text for violating any Fundamental Duty.

This position reflects Parliament's decision to reject the Swaran Singh Committee's recommendation that penalties should accompany the duties.

As a result, Fundamental Duties function primarily as moral, civic, and constitutional obligations rather than legally enforceable commands.

Nevertheless, courts frequently rely upon Fundamental Duties while interpreting laws and constitutional provisions.

Parliament may also enact ordinary legislation that indirectly gives effect to particular duties, such as laws protecting national symbols or the environment.

Therefore, although Fundamental Duties lack direct enforceability, they continue to influence governance, public policy, and constitutional interpretation.

Their significance lies not in judicial compulsion but in fostering civic responsibility and national consciousness among citizens.

National Anthem, National Flag and Sovereignty under Article 51A

Statement: Article 51A(a) requires citizens to respect the Constitution, its institutions, the National Flag and the National Anthem, while Article 51A(c) requires protection of India's sovereignty, unity and integrity.

Among the eleven Fundamental Duties, some are frequently tested in competitive examinations because of their precise constitutional wording.

Under Article 51A(a), every citizen has the duty to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions.

This duty also specifically requires respect for the National Flag and the National Anthem.

An important examination point is that the Constitution explicitly mentions the National Anthem but does not mention the National Song in Article 51A.

Therefore, any statement equating the National Song with the constitutional duty under Article 51A(a) would be incorrect.

The provision seeks to strengthen constitutional patriotism and respect for the symbols representing national unity.

Another important duty appears in Article 51A(c), which requires citizens to uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India.

This obligation emphasizes the responsibility of citizens to preserve the territorial integrity and political independence of the nation.

It reflects the constitutional commitment to national integration and collective responsibility toward the country's security and stability.

Together, Articles 51A(a) and 51A(c) form the core patriotic duties aimed at strengthening national identity and constitutional values.

These provisions remind citizens that respect for national symbols and protection of national unity are essential elements of responsible citizenship.

Article 51A(a)Respect Constitution, institutions, National Flag and National Anthem

UPSC Revision: Article 51A(a) → Respect Constitution, National Flag & National Anthem (not National Song). Article 51A(c) → Uphold sovereignty, unity and integrity of India.

Composite Culture and Environmental Protection under Article 51A

Statement: Article 51A(f) requires citizens to preserve India's composite culture, while Article 51A(g) mandates protection of the environment, including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife.

The Fundamental Duties seek not only to promote patriotism but also to encourage cultural preservation and environmental responsibility.

Article 51A(f) imposes a duty on every citizen to value and preserve the rich heritage of India's composite culture.

The expression 'composite culture' reflects India's long tradition of cultural diversity, coexistence, and mutual influence among different communities, religions, languages, and traditions.

This duty encourages citizens to protect and promote the nation's cultural heritage while respecting its pluralistic character.

Another important duty appears in Article 51A(g), which focuses on environmental conservation.

It requires every citizen to protect and improve the natural environment.

The Constitution specifically mentions forests, lakes, rivers, and wildlife as important components of the natural environment deserving protection.

The provision also directs citizens to show compassion for living creatures, extending environmental responsibility beyond mere conservation of resources.

This duty complements Article 48A, a Directive Principle that places a similar obligation upon the State.

Together, Articles 51A(f) and 51A(g) emphasize that both cultural heritage and ecological balance are shared national responsibilities.

They encourage citizens to contribute actively to preserving India's cultural identity and environmental sustainability for future generations.

Scientific Temper, Humanism and Protection of Public Property

Statement: Article 51A(h) requires citizens to develop scientific temper, humanism, and the spirit of inquiry and reform, while Article 51A(i) mandates safeguarding public property and abjuring violence.

The Fundamental Duties aim not only at preserving national unity and cultural heritage but also at fostering progressive social values among citizens.

Article 51A(h) directs every citizen to develop a scientific temper, along with humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform.

Scientific temper refers to a rational and evidence-based approach toward understanding issues rather than relying solely on superstition, prejudice, or blind belief.

The inclusion of humanism emphasizes respect for human dignity, compassion, and concern for the welfare of others.

The phrase "spirit of inquiry and reform" encourages citizens to question outdated practices, seek knowledge, and support constructive social change.

Another significant duty is contained in Article 51A(i), which requires citizens to safeguard public property.

Public property includes government buildings, roads, public transport systems, schools, hospitals, parks, and other assets created for collective use.

The Constitution further requires citizens to abjure violence, meaning they must reject and renounce violent methods in public and private life.

This provision seeks to protect public resources from vandalism and promote peaceful democratic conduct.

Together, Articles 51A(h) and 51A(i) encourage rational thinking, social responsibility, civic discipline, and peaceful behavior.

They represent the Constitution's vision of an informed, progressive, and responsible citizenry contributing positively to national development.

Verma Committee (1999) and Enforcement of Fundamental Duties

Statement: The Verma Committee (1999) observed that although Fundamental Duties are non-justiciable, numerous statutory laws already exist to penalize conduct that violates these constitutional expectations.

One of the major criticisms of Fundamental Duties has been their non-justiciable nature and the absence of direct constitutional penalties.

To examine whether these duties had any practical legal significance, the Government of India constituted the Justice J.S. Verma Committee on Fundamental Duties in 1999.

The committee studied existing laws and assessed how far they supported the implementation of the duties listed in Article 51A.

After detailed examination, the committee concluded that a substantial network of statutory laws already existed to enforce many of these duties indirectly.

It noted that while courts cannot directly punish a citizen for violating a Fundamental Duty, Parliament has enacted several laws that penalize actions contrary to these constitutional obligations.

Thus, many duties already possess practical legal backing despite lacking direct constitutional enforceability.

The committee emphasized that Fundamental Duties should not be viewed merely as symbolic declarations but as principles supported by various legislative measures.

Its findings demonstrated that constitutional duties and statutory law often work together to promote civic discipline, environmental protection, national unity, and respect for constitutional values.

The Verma Committee therefore helped clarify the relationship between non-justiciable constitutional duties and enforceable legal obligations.

Its observations remain important for understanding how Fundamental Duties operate within the broader constitutional framework.

Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971

Statement: The Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971 provides legal protection to the Constitution, National Flag, and National Anthem by penalizing acts of disrespect against them.

One of the most important laws identified by the Verma Committee as supporting Fundamental Duties is the Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, 1971.

This legislation gives practical effect to the duty contained in Article 51A(a), which requires citizens to respect the Constitution, the National Flag, and the National Anthem.

The Act makes it an offence to intentionally insult or show disrespect toward these national symbols.

Its objective is to preserve the dignity and sanctity of the institutions and symbols that represent the sovereignty and unity of India.

The law specifically protects three important constitutional symbols: the Constitution of India, the National Flag, and the National Anthem.

It provides penalties for acts that deliberately dishonour or insult these symbols in a manner prohibited by law.

The existence of this legislation demonstrates that although Fundamental Duties are non-justiciable, Parliament can enact laws that punish conduct contrary to those duties.

The Act therefore serves as a bridge between constitutional obligations and enforceable legal norms.

It also reinforces the importance of constitutional patriotism and national unity.

For competitive examinations, it is important to remember that the Act is directly linked to the constitutional duty of respecting national symbols under Article 51A(a).

The law remains one of the clearest examples of indirect enforcement of a Fundamental Duty.

UPSC Revision: Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act (1971) → Protects Constitution, National Flag and National Anthem → Linked to Article 51A(a) → Example of indirect enforcement of Fundamental Duties.

Environmental and Social Laws Supporting Fundamental Duties

Statement: Laws such as the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972, Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955, and relevant IPC provisions indirectly enforce several Fundamental Duties relating to environmental protection and social harmony.

The Verma Committee highlighted that a number of statutory laws give practical effect to Fundamental Duties even though Article 51A itself is non-justiciable.

One important category relates to environmental protection under Article 51A(g), which requires citizens to protect and improve the natural environment and show compassion for living creatures.

To support this constitutional objective, Parliament enacted the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972, which provides legal safeguards for wildlife and biodiversity.

Similarly, the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980 helps preserve forests and regulate the diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes.

Together, these laws reinforce citizens' environmental responsibilities and support the constitutional vision of ecological conservation.

The committee also identified laws that promote social harmony and national integration.

The Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 seeks to eliminate discriminatory practices associated with untouchability and promote equality among citizens.

In addition, various provisions of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) penalize acts that promote enmity, hatred, or hostility between different religious, linguistic, regional, or social groups.

These provisions support the constitutional duty of fostering harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood among all Indians.

The existence of such legislation demonstrates that Fundamental Duties are supported by a wide framework of enforceable laws.

Thus, environmental protection, social equality, and communal harmony are not merely constitutional ideals but are reinforced through concrete statutory mechanisms.

LawRelated Fundamental Duty
Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972Protect wildlife and environment
Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980Conserve forests and ecological balance
Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955Promote equality and social harmony
IPC ProvisionsPrevent enmity among religious and linguistic groups
UPSC Revision: Article 51A(g) supported by Wildlife Protection Act (1972) and Forest Conservation Act (1980). Social harmony supported by Protection of Civil Rights Act (1955) and IPC provisions against communal enmity.

Introduction of Constitutional Amendment Bills

Statement: A Constitutional Amendment Bill can be introduced only in Parliament, may be moved by either a minister or private member, and does not require prior Presidential permission.

The procedure for amending the Constitution is primarily governed by Article 368, which lays down the formal mechanism through which constitutional changes can be made.

One of the most important procedural rules is that a Constitutional Amendment Bill can be introduced only in either House of Parliament.

Unlike certain federal constitutions where states may initiate constitutional amendments, the Indian Constitution does not permit state legislatures to introduce an amendment bill.

Thus, the power to initiate constitutional change rests exclusively with Parliament.

The bill may originate in either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha.

There is no constitutional requirement that it must first be introduced in one particular House.

Another notable feature is that the bill may be introduced by either a minister or a private member.

This means that constitutional amendments are not restricted solely to government-sponsored legislation.

Additionally, unlike some categories of bills that require prior Presidential recommendation, a Constitutional Amendment Bill does not require the prior permission of the President before introduction.

These provisions make the initiation process relatively flexible while ensuring that constitutional amendments remain exclusively within the parliamentary framework.

AspectPosition
Governing ProvisionArticle 368
Can be Introduced InEither House of Parliament
Can States Introduce?No
Who May Introduce?Minister or Private Member
Prior Presidential PermissionNot Required
UPSC Revision: Amendment Bill → Only in Parliament → Can originate in Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha → Minister or Private Member can introduce → No role for State Legislatures → No prior Presidential permission required.

No Joint Sitting for Constitutional Amendment Bills

Statement: Constitutional Amendment Bills must be passed separately by both Houses of Parliament, and Article 368 provides no mechanism for a joint sitting in case of disagreement.

The amendment procedure under Article 368 differs significantly from the process followed for ordinary legislation.

For a Constitutional Amendment Bill to succeed, it must be approved independently by both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha with the prescribed majority.

Each House possesses an equal role in the amendment process, regardless of where the bill was originally introduced.

A unique feature of this procedure is the complete absence of any provision for a joint sitting.

Under Article 108 of the Constitution, ordinary bills may be resolved through a joint sitting when a deadlock arises between the two Houses.

However, this mechanism does not apply to Constitutional Amendment Bills.

If one House passes the amendment bill and the other House rejects it, fails to pass it, or proposes unacceptable changes, the bill cannot be rescued through a combined session of Parliament.

In such circumstances, the amendment proposal effectively fails.

This requirement reflects the importance of constitutional amendments and ensures that changes to the Constitution receive independent approval from both Houses.

The Rajya Sabha therefore enjoys equal constitutional authority with the Lok Sabha in matters relating to constitutional amendment.

The absence of a joint sitting mechanism serves as an additional safeguard against hasty or insufficiently supported constitutional changes.

FeaturePosition
Approval RequiredBoth Houses Separately
Joint Sitting Allowed?No
Deadlock Resolution MechanismNone
Status of Bill During DeadlockFails / Cannot Proceed
Position of Rajya SabhaEqual to Lok Sabha
UPSC Revision: Constitutional Amendment Bill → Must pass both Houses separately → No joint sitting under Article 368 → Deadlock cannot be resolved through Article 108 → Rajya Sabha has equal power with Lok Sabha.

Mandatory Presidential Assent to Constitutional Amendments

Statement: Once a Constitutional Amendment Bill is duly passed by Parliament and ratified by states wherever necessary, the President is constitutionally bound to give assent and cannot return or withhold the bill.

The final stage in the constitutional amendment process is the assent of the President of India.

After a Constitutional Amendment Bill has been passed by both Houses of Parliament with the required majority, and after obtaining state ratification wherever constitutionally required, it is presented to the President.

Unlike ordinary bills, the President does not enjoy discretionary authority at this stage.

The President must give assent to the Constitutional Amendment Bill.

There is no constitutional power to withhold assent or exercise a veto.

Similarly, the President cannot return the bill to Parliament for reconsideration.

This position was conclusively established by the 24th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1971.

Before this amendment, some ambiguity existed regarding the extent of the President's role in constitutional amendments.

The 24th Amendment clarified that once Parliament has validly exercised its constituent power under Article 368, Presidential assent becomes mandatory.

This ensures that the constitutional amendment process is not obstructed after all parliamentary and federal requirements have been fulfilled.

The President therefore functions as a constitutional formal authority at this stage rather than as a decision-making authority.

Upon assent, the amendment becomes part of the Constitution and acquires full legal force.

AspectPosition
Final StepPresidential Assent
Can President Withhold Assent?No
Can President Return Bill?No
Made Mandatory By24th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1971
Nature of RoleMandatory Constitutional Assent
UPSC Revision: After passage under Article 368 → President must give assent → Cannot withhold or return the bill → 24th Constitutional Amendment Act (1971) made assent mandatory.

Amendments Outside Article 368 – Simple Majority Method

Statement: Certain constitutional provisions can be amended by a simple majority of Parliament and are specifically kept outside the scope of Article 368.

The Constitution does not follow a single procedure for all amendments. Instead, different provisions require different levels of parliamentary approval.

The simplest category consists of provisions that can be amended by a simple majority of members present and voting, the same procedure generally used for ordinary legislation.

These amendments are not regarded as amendments under the strict framework of Article 368.

Consequently, they are often described as amendments that are outside Article 368.

The Constitution deliberately places certain administrative and structural matters in this category to provide flexibility and facilitate governance.

Examples include the admission or establishment of new states, formation of new states, alteration of state boundaries, and changes in state names.

Other examples include provisions relating to official languages, parliamentary quorum requirements, and certain matters concerning legislative procedure.

Because these changes do not affect the core constitutional framework, Parliament is permitted to modify them through a relatively easier procedure.

This arrangement reflects the partly rigid and partly flexible nature of the Indian Constitution.

Thus, while some constitutional provisions demand special majorities or state ratification, others can be changed through an ordinary legislative majority.

The existence of this category enables the Constitution to adapt efficiently to administrative and territorial changes without invoking the more rigorous amendment process under Article 368.

CategoryRequirement
Simple Majority AmendmentMajority of members present and voting
Covered by Article 368?No
ExamplesNew states, state boundaries, official languages, quorum provisions
NatureMost flexible amendment procedure
UPSC Revision: Simple Majority Amendments → Outside Article 368 → Same as ordinary legislation → Used for new states, state boundaries, official languages, quorum and related matters.

Special Majority Amendments under Article 368 (Type 1)

Statement: Most constitutional provisions are amended through a Special Majority requiring both a majority of the total membership of each House and a two-thirds majority of members present and voting.

The most commonly used amendment procedure under Article 368 is the Special Majority method, often referred to as the first category of constitutional amendments.

This procedure is more rigorous than a simple majority because constitutional provisions are considered more important than ordinary laws.

To pass such an amendment, each House of Parliament must satisfy two separate conditions simultaneously.

First, the bill must secure the support of a majority of the total membership of that House.

Second, it must obtain the support of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

Both conditions are mandatory and must be fulfilled independently in the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.

This special majority is often described as an effective majority plus a two-thirds majority of those present and voting.

The procedure applies to the amendment of a large number of constitutional provisions, including the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles of State Policy.

It also governs amendments relating to most constitutional provisions that do not fall within either the simple majority category or the federal ratification category.

The requirement of a special majority ensures that important constitutional changes enjoy broad parliamentary support and cannot be made by a narrow or temporary political majority.

As a result, this method balances constitutional stability with the need for periodic reform.

RequirementCondition
Condition 1Majority of total membership of each House
Condition 2At least two-thirds of members present and voting
Applicable ToFundamental Rights, DPSPs and most constitutional provisions
State Ratification Required?No
UPSC Revision: Article 368 Special Majority = Majority of total membership + Two-thirds of members present and voting in each House. Used for Fundamental Rights, DPSPs and most constitutional provisions.

Federal Amendment Procedure – Special Majority Plus State Ratification (Type 2)

Statement: Amendments affecting the federal structure require a Special Majority in Parliament and ratification by at least one-half of the State Legislatures.

The most rigorous amendment procedure under the Constitution is reserved for provisions that affect the federal balance between the Union and the States.

Because such matters concern both levels of government, Parliament alone cannot amend them.

These amendments require the same Special Majority under Article 368 that applies to most constitutional amendments.

Accordingly, the bill must be passed in each House by a majority of the total membership and by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

However, parliamentary approval alone is insufficient.

After being passed by Parliament, the amendment must also be ratified by the legislatures of at least one-half of the States.

The ratification in each State Legislature is done by a simple majority of members present and voting.

No special majority is required at the state level.

This additional requirement ensures that changes affecting India's federal structure cannot be imposed solely by the Union Parliament.

Important examples include amendments relating to the election of the President, the extent of the powers of the Supreme Court and High Courts, distribution of legislative powers between the Union and the States, and any modification of the Seventh Schedule.

Thus, this procedure protects federalism by giving states a direct role in constitutional changes that affect their constitutional position.

StageRequirement
ParliamentSpecial Majority under Article 368
StatesRatification by at least half of State Legislatures
State Voting RequirementSimple Majority
ExamplesPresident's election, Supreme Court/High Court powers, Seventh Schedule, federal distribution of powers
UPSC Revision: Federal Amendments = Special Majority in Parliament + Ratification by at least 50% of States (simple majority). Applies to President's election, judiciary powers, federal relations and Seventh Schedule provisions.

Shankari Prasad and Sajjan Singh Cases: The Early Judicial Position

Statement: In the Shankari Prasad (1951) and Sajjan Singh (1965) cases, the Supreme Court held that Parliament possesses the power to amend Fundamental Rights and that a Constitutional Amendment is not a 'law' under Article 13.

The debate over Parliament's power to amend the Constitution began soon after independence, particularly regarding whether Fundamental Rights could be altered through constitutional amendments.

The first major judicial pronouncement came in the case of Shankari Prasad v. Union of India (1951).

The Supreme Court examined the validity of the First Constitutional Amendment Act and considered whether Parliament could amend Fundamental Rights.

The Court ruled that the power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 includes the power to amend any part of the Constitution, including Part III containing Fundamental Rights.

It further held that a Constitutional Amendment Act is not a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13.

Since Article 13 declares laws violating Fundamental Rights to be void, the Court reasoned that constitutional amendments were outside its scope.

This interpretation gave Parliament broad authority to modify Fundamental Rights through the constitutional amendment process.

The same principle was reaffirmed in Sajjan Singh v. State of Rajasthan (1965).

In that case, the Supreme Court again upheld Parliament's amending power and maintained that Article 368 authorizes amendments to Fundamental Rights.

Thus, during the period between 1951 and 1965, the judicial position strongly favored parliamentary supremacy in the field of constitutional amendments.

These decisions laid the foundation for the later constitutional conflict that eventually culminated in the Golaknath and Kesavananda Bharati cases.

CaseYearDecision
Shankari Prasad1951Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights
Sajjan Singh1965Reaffirmed Shankari Prasad
Article 13 Status—Constitutional Amendment is not 'law'
UPSC Revision: Shankari Prasad (1951) + Sajjan Singh (1965) → Parliament can amend Fundamental Rights → Constitutional Amendment not considered 'law' under Article 13.

Golaknath Case (1967): The Great Reversal

Statement: In Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967), the Supreme Court reversed its earlier decisions and held that Parliament cannot amend or abridge Fundamental Rights.

The constitutional debate over Parliament's amending power reached a dramatic turning point in the landmark case of I.C. Golaknath v. State of Punjab (1967).

An 11-judge Constitution Bench reconsidered the earlier rulings delivered in Shankari Prasad and Sajjan Singh.

By a narrow majority, the Supreme Court departed from its previous interpretation and adopted a completely different constitutional position.

The Court held that Fundamental Rights occupy a transcendental and immutable position within the constitutional framework.

According to the majority, Parliament did not possess the authority to take away, abridge, or curtail any Fundamental Right through the amendment process.

The Court reasoned that constitutional amendments affecting Fundamental Rights would fall within the scope of Article 13 and therefore could be subjected to constitutional scrutiny.

As a result, Parliament's amending power was significantly restricted.

The judgment effectively placed Fundamental Rights beyond the reach of constitutional amendments.

This decision created a direct confrontation between the judiciary and Parliament because several socio-economic reforms depended upon constitutional amendments affecting property and related rights.

In response, Parliament enacted a series of constitutional amendments, including the 24th Constitutional Amendment Act (1971), to restore and clarify its amending authority.

The Golaknath judgment therefore became a crucial stage in the constitutional struggle over the limits of Parliament's power.

Its ruling remained the governing position until it was reconsidered by the Supreme Court in the historic Kesavananda Bharati case of 1973.

AspectPosition in Golaknath (1967)
Bench Strength11 Judges
Status of Fundamental RightsTranscendental and Immutable
Can Parliament Amend FRs?No
Effect on Earlier CasesOverruled Shankari Prasad and Sajjan Singh
Later FateOverruled by Kesavananda Bharati (1973)
UPSC Revision: Golaknath (1967) → 11-judge bench → Fundamental Rights are transcendental and immutable → Parliament cannot amend or abridge FRs → Overruled earlier cases → Later overruled by Kesavananda Bharati (1973).

Kesavananda Bharati Case (1973): Birth of the Basic Structure Doctrine

Statement: In Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973), the Supreme Court held that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, but cannot destroy or damage its Basic Structure.

The most significant constitutional judgment in Indian history was delivered in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973).

The case was heard by a historic 13-judge Constitution Bench, the largest bench ever constituted by the Supreme Court of India.

The central question before the Court was whether Parliament possessed unlimited power to amend the Constitution.

The judgment struck a middle path between the views expressed in the earlier cases.

The Court rejected the Golaknath position that Fundamental Rights were completely beyond amendment.

At the same time, it also rejected the argument that Parliament's amending power was unlimited.

The Supreme Court held that Parliament may amend any provision of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights contained in Part III.

However, such amendments cannot alter, destroy, damage, or emasculate the Constitution's Basic Structure.

This principle became known as the Basic Structure Doctrine, one of the most influential constitutional doctrines in the world.

The Court did not provide an exhaustive list of what constitutes the Basic Structure, leaving its identification to future judicial interpretation.

The doctrine ensured a balance between constitutional flexibility and constitutional permanence.

As a result, Parliament retained broad amending authority, but that authority became subject to substantive constitutional limitations.

The Kesavananda judgment remains the foundation of modern Indian constitutional law and continues to govern every constitutional amendment today.

AspectKesavananda Bharati (1973)
Bench Strength13 Judges
Can Parliament Amend FRs?Yes
Amending Power Unlimited?No
Doctrine CreatedBasic Structure Doctrine
Core PrincipleBasic Structure cannot be damaged or destroyed
UPSC Revision: Kesavananda Bharati (1973) → 13-judge bench → Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution including FRs → Cannot destroy Basic Structure → Birth of the Basic Structure Doctrine.

Indira Nehru Gandhi Case (1975): First Major Application of the Basic Structure Doctrine

Statement: In the Indira Nehru Gandhi Case (1975), the Supreme Court applied the Basic Structure Doctrine to strike down provisions of the 39th Constitutional Amendment that sought to exclude certain election disputes from judicial scrutiny.

The Basic Structure Doctrine announced in Kesavananda Bharati was tested almost immediately in the politically significant case of Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975).

The dispute arose after the election of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was challenged before the courts.

In response, Parliament enacted the 39th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1975.

The amendment attempted to place disputes relating to the election of the Prime Minister, President, Vice-President, and Speaker of the Lok Sabha beyond the jurisdiction of ordinary courts.

Essentially, it sought to remove judicial scrutiny over certain high constitutional offices.

The Supreme Court examined whether such an amendment was compatible with the Constitution's Basic Structure.

The Court held that Parliament could not use its amending power to eliminate fundamental constitutional principles.

It struck down the offending provisions of the 39th Amendment and reaffirmed that Judicial Review is an essential feature of the Constitution.

The judgment also emphasized the importance of free and fair elections as a foundational element of democratic governance.

By invalidating parts of the amendment, the Court demonstrated that the Basic Structure Doctrine was not merely a theoretical concept but a practical limitation on constitutional amendments.

This case therefore became the first major example of the doctrine being actively used to invalidate a constitutional amendment.

It strengthened the role of the judiciary as the guardian of constitutional fundamentals and reinforced the supremacy of the Constitution over transient political interests.

AspectPosition
CaseIndira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975)
Amendment Challenged39th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1975
IssueExclusion of election disputes from court jurisdiction
Basic Structure Feature AppliedJudicial Review
ResultRelevant provisions struck down
UPSC Revision: Indira Nehru Gandhi Case (1975) → 39th Amendment challenged → Election disputes cannot be placed beyond courts → Judicial Review and Free & Fair Elections recognized as Basic Structure elements.

Minerva Mills Case (1980): Limited Amending Power as Basic Structure

Statement: In Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980), the Supreme Court struck down provisions of the 42nd Amendment and held that Parliament's limited amending power itself forms part of the Basic Structure.

Following the Kesavananda Bharati judgment, Parliament attempted to strengthen its constitutional amendment authority through the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976.

Among its various provisions, the amendment sought to expand Parliament's amending power and reduce the scope of judicial scrutiny over constitutional amendments.

In particular, it attempted to give Parliament virtually unlimited authority to amend any part of the Constitution.

These provisions were challenged before the Supreme Court in the landmark case of Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980).

The Court examined whether Parliament could enlarge its own powers to such an extent that constitutional limitations would effectively disappear.

Relying on the Basic Structure Doctrine established in Kesavananda Bharati, the Supreme Court struck down the relevant provisions of the 42nd Amendment.

The Court declared that Parliament's amending power is limited and that this limitation itself constitutes a Basic Structure feature.

According to the judgment, if Parliament were permitted unlimited amendment authority, it could potentially destroy the Constitution's essential identity.

The Court therefore held that the Constitution creates a balance between flexibility and permanence.

Another significant contribution of the judgment was its emphasis on the harmony and balance between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles of State Policy.

The Court observed that neither Part III nor Part IV is superior; both must coexist in a balanced constitutional framework.

The Minerva Mills decision thus reinforced judicial review, preserved constitutional limitations, and further strengthened the Basic Structure Doctrine.

AspectPosition
CaseMinerva Mills v. Union of India (1980)
Amendment Challenged42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976
Key PrincipleLimited amending power is Basic Structure
Additional Basic FeatureHarmony between FRs and DPSPs
OutcomeRelevant provisions struck down
UPSC Revision: Minerva Mills (1980) → Struck down parts of 42nd Amendment → Parliament's limited amending power is Basic Structure → Harmony between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs is also Basic Structure.

Waman Rao Case (1981) and the Ninth Schedule Cut-Off Date

Statement: In Waman Rao v. Union of India (1981), the Supreme Court held that laws placed in the Ninth Schedule after 24 April 1973 are subject to judicial review if they violate the Basic Structure of the Constitution.

The scope of the Basic Structure Doctrine required further clarification after the Kesavananda Bharati judgment.

A major question arose regarding laws protected under the Ninth Schedule, which had historically been used to shield certain statutes from judicial scrutiny.

This issue came before the Supreme Court in Waman Rao v. Union of India (1981).

The Court was required to determine whether the Basic Structure Doctrine should apply retrospectively to laws already placed in the Ninth Schedule before the Kesavananda decision.

The Supreme Court adopted a balanced approach and introduced an important constitutional cut-off date.

It held that the Basic Structure Doctrine would operate prospectively rather than retrospectively.

The decisive date was fixed as 24 April 1973, the day on which the Kesavananda Bharati judgment was delivered.

Accordingly, laws inserted into the Ninth Schedule before this date would generally remain protected from challenge on the ground of violating the Basic Structure.

However, any law placed in the Ninth Schedule after 24 April 1973 could be examined by the courts.

If such a law damaged or destroyed a Basic Structure feature, it could be declared unconstitutional.

The judgment therefore reinforced the supremacy of the Constitution while avoiding large-scale disruption of earlier legal arrangements.

Waman Rao became a crucial milestone in defining the temporal application of the Basic Structure Doctrine and preserving judicial review over future constitutional actions.

AspectPosition
CaseWaman Rao v. Union of India (1981)
Doctrine AppliedBasic Structure Doctrine
Nature of ApplicationProspective
Cut-Off Date24 April 1973
Post-Cut-Off Ninth Schedule LawsSubject to Judicial Review
UPSC Revision: Waman Rao (1981) → Basic Structure Doctrine applies prospectively → Cut-off date = 24 April 1973 (Kesavananda judgment) → Ninth Schedule laws added after this date can be reviewed by courts.

The Electoral College of the President of India

Statement: The President of India is elected indirectly by a specially constituted Electoral College consisting only of elected representatives from Parliament and certain Legislative Assemblies.

The President of India is not elected directly by the people. Instead, the Constitution provides for an indirect election through an Electoral College to preserve the federal character of the Union while ensuring broad democratic legitimacy.

The Electoral College consists of the elected members of both Houses of Parliament, namely the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.

In addition, it includes the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of all States.

Following constitutional developments relating to Union Territories, the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of Delhi, Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir also participate in the Presidential election.

A crucial examination point is that only elected members are included.

Therefore, nominated members of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha do not participate in the Presidential election.

Similarly, members of State Legislative Councils (MLCs) are excluded because the Constitution restricts participation to elected members of Legislative Assemblies.

This ensures that the President is chosen by representatives who themselves possess a direct electoral mandate.

The composition of the Electoral College reflects a balance between the Union and the States, making the office of the President representative of the entire federal structure.

For UPSC examinations, the most frequent trap is confusing the Presidential Electoral College with that of the Vice-President, where nominated MPs are included and State MLAs are excluded.

Included in Electoral CollegeExcluded from Electoral College
Elected Lok Sabha MPsNominated Lok Sabha Members
Elected Rajya Sabha MPsNominated Rajya Sabha Members
Elected State MLAsState MLCs
Elected MLAs of Delhi, Puducherry & J&KAll unelected members
UPSC Revision: Presidential Electoral College = Elected MPs (Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha) + Elected MLAs of States + Elected MLAs of Delhi, Puducherry & J&K. Nominated MPs and all MLCs do NOT participate.

Value of Votes and the Electoral System for Presidential Elections

Statement: The President is elected through proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote, and the value of each MLA's vote is determined using the 1971 Census population figures.

The Presidential election in India follows a unique electoral system designed to balance representation between the Union and the States.

The election is conducted through the method of Proportional Representation by means of the Single Transferable Vote (STV).

Voting takes place through a secret ballot, enabling electors to vote according to their preference without being bound by a party whip.

Unlike ordinary elections where each vote carries equal value, the Presidential election assigns different vote values to electors.

The purpose of this arrangement is to ensure parity between the States and the Union while reflecting differences in state population.

The value of an MLA's vote is calculated using the population of the respective State as recorded in the 1971 Census.

This constitutional freeze on population figures was introduced to ensure that States successfully implementing population-control measures were not disadvantaged in national representation.

After determining the value of MLA votes, the value of each MP's vote is calculated in such a way that the total value of votes of all MPs roughly equals the total value of votes of all MLAs combined.

The Single Transferable Vote system allows electors to rank candidates according to preference rather than voting for only one candidate.

If no candidate secures the required quota in the initial count, lower-preference votes are transferred according to the prescribed rules until a winner emerges.

This system ensures broad-based support and enhances the representative legitimacy of the office of the President.

FeaturePosition
Election MethodProportional Representation
Voting SystemSingle Transferable Vote (STV)
Voting ModeSecret Ballot
Basis for MLA Vote Value1971 Census Population
ObjectiveUniform representation of States and Union
UPSC Revision: President Election → Proportional Representation + Single Transferable Vote + Secret Ballot. MLA vote value is based on the 1971 Census population; MP vote values are adjusted to maintain Union-State balance.

Impeachment of the President (Article 61)

Statement: The President can be removed only through impeachment for 'Violation of the Constitution', and the process requires a two-thirds majority of the total membership of both Houses of Parliament.

The Constitution provides a special and rigorous procedure for removing the President from office in order to preserve the dignity and stability of the highest constitutional office.

Under Article 61, the President may be impeached only on the ground of 'Violation of the Constitution'.

A noteworthy constitutional feature is that the expression 'Violation of the Constitution' has not been defined anywhere in the Constitution.

This leaves its interpretation to Parliament during impeachment proceedings.

The impeachment process may be initiated in either House of Parliament.

Before a resolution is moved, a written notice signed by at least one-fourth of the total members of the House must be given, and a minimum notice period of fourteen days must be observed.

If the initiating House passes the impeachment resolution, the other House investigates the charge or causes it to be investigated.

The President has the right to appear and be represented during the investigation.

For the impeachment to succeed, the resolution must be passed by not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House considering it.

Since both Houses must separately pass the resolution by this stringent majority, impeachment is extremely difficult and intended only for exceptional circumstances.

A major UPSC trap concerns participation in the process: nominated members of Parliament do participate in impeachment proceedings because they are members of Parliament, whereas State MLAs play no role whatsoever.

Once both Houses pass the resolution, the President stands removed from office from the date on which the second House adopts the resolution.

AspectProvision
ArticleArticle 61
Ground for RemovalViolation of the Constitution
Defined in Constitution?No
InitiationEither House of Parliament
Majority Required2/3 of Total Membership of Each House
Nominated MPs Participate?Yes
State MLAs Participate?No
UPSC Revision: President can be impeached only for 'Violation of the Constitution' (undefined term). Requires 2/3 of the TOTAL membership of both Houses. Nominated MPs participate; State MLAs do not.

Article 74 and the Binding Advice of the Council of Ministers

Statement: Article 74 requires the President to act according to the advice of the Council of Ministers, with the 44th Amendment permitting only a one-time reconsideration of that advice.

India follows the Parliamentary form of government, where the President serves as the constitutional head while the real executive authority rests with the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.

This principle is embodied in Article 74, which provides that there shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at its head to aid and advise the President.

In the original constitutional framework, the extent to which such advice was binding remained a matter of debate.

To remove ambiguity, the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 explicitly provided that the President shall act in accordance with the advice of the Council of Ministers.

This amendment effectively made ministerial advice absolutely binding upon the President.

However, concerns arose that the President should possess at least a limited opportunity to caution the government against potentially undesirable decisions.

Accordingly, the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978 modified the arrangement.

It empowered the President to return the advice once for reconsideration by the Council of Ministers.

This allows the President to request a fresh review of the matter without directly obstructing the executive process.

Nevertheless, if the Council of Ministers reconsiders the issue and sends back the same advice, the President is constitutionally bound to accept it.

Thus, the President possesses only a suspensive opportunity for reconsideration and not a veto over executive advice.

The provision preserves democratic accountability by ensuring that elected ministers ultimately determine executive policy while still allowing the President to offer constitutional caution.

AmendmentEffect on Presidential Advice
Original ConstitutionBinding nature not expressly clarified
42nd Amendment (1976)Made advice absolutely binding
44th Amendment (1978)President may return advice once
After ReconsiderationPresident must accept advice
UPSC Revision: Article 74 → President acts on Council of Ministers' advice. 42nd Amendment made advice binding. 44th Amendment allows one-time reconsideration, but reconsidered advice must be accepted.

President's Pardoning Power under Article 72

Statement: The President's pardoning power under Article 72 is broader than the Governor's power under Article 161, particularly regarding Court Martial cases and death sentences.

Article 72 empowers the President of India to grant clemency in appropriate cases as part of the constitutional justice system.

This power serves as a humanitarian safeguard against possible judicial errors and allows consideration of factors beyond strict legal rules.

The President may grant different forms of relief including Pardon, Reprieve, Respite, Remission, and Commutation.

A Pardon completely absolves the offender from both conviction and punishment.

A Reprieve temporarily postpones the execution of a sentence, particularly a death sentence.

A Respite awards a lesser sentence due to special circumstances such as physical disability or pregnancy.

A Remission reduces the period of punishment without changing its nature, whereas a Commutation substitutes one form of punishment with a lighter one.

The President's clemency jurisdiction extends to cases involving punishment by a Court Martial, a power that is not available to State Governors.

Another significant distinction concerns death sentences.

The President possesses the power to completely pardon a death sentence and erase the punishment altogether.

In contrast, although a Governor may suspend, remit, or commute a death sentence, the Governor cannot grant a complete pardon in such cases.

Because of these two additional powers—Court Martial jurisdiction and complete pardon of death sentences—the President's pardoning authority is regarded as constitutionally broader than that of a Governor.

AreaPresident (Article 72)Governor (Article 161)
Court Martial CasesYesNo
Complete Pardon of Death SentenceYesNo
Suspend/Remit/Commute Death SentenceYesYes
UPSC Revision: President's pardoning power is wider than Governor's because: (1) President can grant relief in Court Martial cases, and (2) President can completely pardon a death sentence. Governor cannot do either.

Vice-President's Electoral College: Key Contrast with the President

Statement: The Vice-President is elected by an Electoral College consisting of both elected and nominated members of Parliament, while State Legislatures play no role.

The method of electing the Vice-President differs significantly from that used for electing the President of India.

While both offices are filled through indirect elections based on proportional representation and the single transferable vote system, the composition of their Electoral Colleges is fundamentally different.

The Vice-President is elected by an Electoral College consisting of all members of both Houses of Parliament.

This includes not only elected members of the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha but also nominated members of both Houses.

The inclusion of nominated members is one of the most frequently tested distinctions in UPSC examinations.

Another important feature is that State Legislative Assemblies have absolutely no role in the Vice-Presidential election.

Neither elected MLAs nor members of Legislative Councils participate in the process.

This differs sharply from the Presidential election, where elected MLAs form a substantial component of the Electoral College.

The exclusion of State Legislatures reflects the constitutional position of the Vice-President as a functionary closely connected with Parliament, particularly the Rajya Sabha.

The election is conducted through proportional representation by means of the Single Transferable Vote (STV) and voting occurs through a secret ballot.

Because only Parliament participates, the Vice-President is essentially elected by the national legislature rather than by a federal Electoral College.

FeaturePresidentVice-President
Elected MPsIncludedIncluded
Nominated MPsExcludedIncluded
State MLAsIncludedExcluded
MLCsExcludedExcluded
UPSC Revision: VP Electoral College = All MPs (elected + nominated). State MLAs do not participate. This is the exact opposite trap compared to the Presidential Electoral College.

Vice-President as the Ex-Officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha

Statement: Under Article 64, the Vice-President serves as the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and receives remuneration for that role rather than for holding the office of Vice-President.

The Constitution assigns a unique parliamentary role to the Vice-President of India.

According to Article 64, the Vice-President functions as the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, the Upper House of Parliament.

The expression ex-officio means that the office is held by virtue of another office.

Therefore, whoever becomes Vice-President automatically assumes the position of Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

As the presiding officer of the Rajya Sabha, the Vice-President regulates debates, maintains order, interprets procedural rules, and ensures the smooth conduct of House proceedings.

He decides who may speak, supervises voting procedures, and safeguards parliamentary discipline.

Unlike the Speaker of the Lok Sabha, the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha is not a member of the House.

Consequently, he ordinarily does not participate in voting on motions and resolutions.

However, when votes are equally divided, he may exercise a casting vote to break the tie.

A frequently tested constitutional point concerns remuneration.

The Vice-President does not draw a salary as Vice-President.

Instead, while performing the duties of presiding over the Rajya Sabha, he receives salary and allowances as the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

This arrangement highlights the close institutional relationship between the office of the Vice-President and the Upper House of Parliament.

AspectProvision
ArticleArticle 64
Position HeldEx-Officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha
Member of Rajya Sabha?No
Ordinary Voting RightNo
Casting Vote in TieYes
Salary Drawn AsChairman of Rajya Sabha
UPSC Revision: Article 64 → Vice-President is Ex-Officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha. Draws salary as Chairman, not as Vice-President. Has no ordinary vote but can exercise a casting vote in case of a tie.

Removal of the Vice-President (Article 67)

Statement: The Vice-President can be removed by a resolution of the Rajya Sabha passed by an Effective Majority and agreed to by the Lok Sabha through a Simple Majority. No constitutional grounds for removal are specified.

The Constitution provides a distinct procedure for the removal of the Vice-President, which is considerably simpler than the impeachment process applicable to the President.

Under Article 67, the Vice-President may be removed from office through a resolution initiated in the Rajya Sabha.

The Constitution does not prescribe any specific grounds such as misconduct, incapacity, or violation of the Constitution.

Therefore, unlike the President, whose removal requires impeachment on a specified ground, the Vice-President can be removed without proving any constitutionally defined offence.

The removal resolution must first be passed by the Rajya Sabha, where the Vice-President serves as the ex-officio Chairman.

The required majority is an Effective Majority, meaning a majority of all the then members of the House, excluding vacant seats.

After being passed by the Rajya Sabha, the resolution must be agreed to by the Lok Sabha.

In the Lok Sabha, only a Simple Majority of members present and voting is required.

The Constitution also requires that at least 14 days' notice be given before moving the removal resolution.

This procedure reflects the Vice-President's close constitutional association with the Rajya Sabha.

A major UPSC distinction is that the President is removed through impeachment requiring a two-thirds majority of the total membership of both Houses, whereas the Vice-President's removal follows a comparatively less stringent parliamentary procedure.

The absence of specified removal grounds and the use of an Effective Majority make Article 67 a frequently tested topic in constitutional examinations.

AspectProvision
ArticleArticle 67
Initiating HouseRajya Sabha
Rajya Sabha MajorityEffective Majority
Lok Sabha ApprovalSimple Majority
Notice Period14 Days
Specified Grounds?No
UPSC Revision: Article 67 → VP removed by Rajya Sabha (Effective Majority) + Lok Sabha (Simple Majority). 14 days' notice required. No grounds for removal are specified in the Constitution.

President and Prime Minister: Nominal vs Real Executive

Statement: The President is the nominal (de jure) executive head of India, while the Prime Minister is the real (de facto) executive head and principal link between the Council of Ministers and the President.

India follows the Parliamentary system of government modeled largely on the British constitutional framework.

One of its defining features is the distinction between the nominal executive and the real executive.

The President of India serves as the constitutional or nominal executive head, often described as the De Jure Executive.

In legal theory, all executive actions of the Union are carried out in the name of the President.

However, the President ordinarily exercises these powers on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Prime Minister.

The Prime Minister, therefore, functions as the De Facto Executive or real executive authority of the Union Government.

The Prime Minister leads the Council of Ministers, determines government policies, coordinates administrative action, and directs the functioning of the executive branch.

Under Article 78, the Prime Minister acts as the principal channel of communication between the President and the Council of Ministers.

It is the Prime Minister's constitutional duty to keep the President informed regarding decisions of the Council of Ministers and matters relating to the administration of Union affairs.

The Prime Minister must also furnish information sought by the President and place before the Council any matter that the President requires to be considered.

This arrangement ensures that the President remains informed while preserving the principle that real executive authority rests with an elected political leadership accountable to Parliament.

The distinction between the President as De Jure Executive and the Prime Minister as De Facto Executive is one of the most fundamental concepts of Indian constitutional governance.

AspectPresidentPrime Minister
StatusNominal ExecutiveReal Executive
NatureDe Jure HeadDe Facto Head
Executive AuthorityFormalActual
Article 78 RoleReceives InformationCommunicates Decisions
UPSC Revision: President = De Jure (Nominal) Executive. Prime Minister = De Facto (Real) Executive. Article 78 makes the PM the constitutional link between the President and the Council of Ministers.

Collective Responsibility of the Council of Ministers (Article 75)

Statement: Under Article 75, the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Lok Sabha, and a successful No-Confidence Motion requires the entire Ministry to resign.

The principle of Collective Responsibility is one of the cornerstones of the Parliamentary system of government in India.

It is expressly embodied in Article 75(3), which states that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

This means that the Ministry functions as a unified body and stands or falls together.

Every minister, whether inside or outside the Cabinet, is expected to publicly support government decisions.

If a minister disagrees fundamentally with a policy decision, constitutional convention requires resignation rather than public opposition.

A critical examination point is that the Council of Ministers is responsible only to the Lok Sabha and not to Parliament as a whole.

The Rajya Sabha may discuss government policies and hold ministers accountable through debates and questions, but it cannot remove the government from office.

The most important test of collective responsibility is the No-Confidence Motion in the Lok Sabha.

If the Lok Sabha passes a No-Confidence Motion against the government, it signifies that the Ministry has lost the confidence of the directly elected House.

In such a situation, the entire Council of Ministers must resign collectively.

This obligation extends to all ministers, including those who may be members of the Rajya Sabha.

Thus, even a Rajya Sabha minister loses office when the government loses majority support in the Lok Sabha.

The doctrine ensures unified executive accountability and preserves democratic control of the executive through the popularly elected chamber.

AspectProvision
ArticleArticle 75(3)
Responsible ToLok Sabha Only
Responsible To Rajya Sabha?No
Test of ResponsibilityNo-Confidence Motion
