Applies to Rajya Sabha Ministers?Yes
UPSC Revision: Article 75(3) → Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Lok Sabha. If a No-Confidence Motion passes, the entire Ministry—including Rajya Sabha ministers—must resign.

Individual Responsibility of Ministers and the Pleasure Doctrine

Statement: Ministers hold office during the pleasure of the President, but in practice a minister can be removed only on the advice of the Prime Minister.

Alongside collective responsibility, the Constitution also recognizes the principle of Individual Responsibility of ministers.

This principle is embodied in Article 75, which provides that ministers shall hold office during the pleasure of the President.

At first glance, this provision appears to give the President unrestricted authority to dismiss any minister.

However, under the Parliamentary system, constitutional powers are exercised in accordance with the advice of the Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers.

Consequently, the President does not independently decide whether a minister should continue in office.

If the Prime Minister loses confidence in a minister or wishes to reorganize the Ministry, he may advise the President to remove that minister.

Upon receiving such advice, the President formally dismisses the minister concerned.

Therefore, while the Constitution speaks of the President's pleasure, the practical and political control over ministerial appointments and removals rests with the Prime Minister.

This arrangement strengthens the Prime Minister's leadership position within the Council of Ministers.

It ensures cabinet discipline because ministers remain accountable not only to Parliament but also to the Prime Minister who heads the government.

The doctrine also prevents the President from interfering directly in day-to-day political management of the executive.

As a result, the phrase 'pleasure of the President' operates largely as a constitutional formality, whereas the real authority behind ministerial removal lies with the Prime Minister.

AspectPosition
Relevant ArticleArticle 75
Formal PositionMinisters hold office during President's pleasure
Actual Decision MakerPrime Minister
Can President Remove Minister Independently?No
PurposeEnsures PM's leadership and cabinet discipline
UPSC Revision: Ministers hold office during the President's pleasure, but the President acts on the Prime Minister's advice. Real power to remove a minister rests with the Prime Minister.

The 15% Ceiling on the Union Council of Ministers

Statement: The total strength of the Union Council of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, cannot exceed 15% of the total membership of the Lok Sabha.

To prevent excessively large ministries and promote efficient governance, the Constitution places a numerical limit on the size of the Union Council of Ministers.

This restriction was introduced through the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003.

According to Article 75, the total number of ministers in the Union Government, including the Prime Minister, shall not exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.

Before this amendment, there was no constitutional ceiling on the number of ministers.

As a result, governments occasionally created very large ministries for political accommodation, coalition management, or distribution of offices.

The 91st Amendment sought to curb this trend and reduce the possibility of political patronage through ministerial appointments.

The amendment also aimed to improve administrative efficiency by ensuring that ministries remain compact and functional.

The Prime Minister is included while calculating the 15% limit.

Since the present sanctioned strength of the Lok Sabha is 545 members, the constitutional ceiling is calculated with reference to the total strength of the House and not the number of members actually present.

This provision applies uniformly irrespective of whether the government is a single-party administration or a coalition government.

A similar constitutional restriction also exists for State Councils of Ministers under Article 164, though the detailed provisions differ.

The 15% cap remains one of the most frequently tested constitutional amendments relating to executive organization.

AspectProvision
Constitutional Amendment91st Amendment Act, 2003
Applies ToUnion Council of Ministers
Maximum Size15% of Lok Sabha Strength
Prime Minister Included?Yes
PurposePrevent oversized ministries and political patronage
UPSC Revision: 91st Amendment (2003) → Total number of Union Ministers, including the PM, cannot exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.

Three-Tier Structure of the Council of Ministers and the Constitutional Status of the Cabinet

Statement: The Council of Ministers consists of different ranks of ministers, while the term 'Cabinet' entered the Constitution only through the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978.

The Council of Ministers is a broad constitutional body headed by the Prime Minister and entrusted with aiding and advising the President.

Within this larger body exists a smaller and more powerful group known as the Cabinet, which serves as the principal decision-making authority of the Union Government.

Traditionally, the Council of Ministers is organized into a three-tier hierarchy.

The highest level consists of Cabinet Ministers, who head major ministries and participate directly in cabinet meetings where crucial policy decisions are taken.

The second category comprises Ministers of State. Some function independently with charge of specific departments, while others assist Cabinet Ministers in larger ministries.

The third category consists of Deputy Ministers, who assist senior ministers in administrative and parliamentary work.

Although all these categories together form the Council of Ministers, only Cabinet Ministers ordinarily participate in formal cabinet deliberations.

An important constitutional fact is that the word 'Cabinet' did not originally appear anywhere in the Constitution of India as adopted in 1950.

Its constitutional recognition came through the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978.

This amendment inserted the term into Article 352, which deals with the proclamation of a National Emergency.

The purpose was to ensure that such a proclamation could be issued only after a written recommendation of the Cabinet and not merely on the advice of an individual minister.

Thus, while the Cabinet had always existed in practice, the 44th Amendment gave it explicit constitutional recognition.

LevelRole
Cabinet MinistersHead major ministries and make key policy decisions

UPSC Revision: Council of Ministers = Cabinet Ministers + Ministers of State + Deputy Ministers. The term 'Cabinet' was absent in the original Constitution and was inserted into Article 352 by the 44th Amendment Act (1978).

Attorney General for India: Constitutional Position and Qualifications

Statement: The Attorney General for India is the highest law officer of the Union and must possess qualifications required for appointment as a Judge of the Supreme Court.

The Attorney General for India is the chief legal advisor to the Government of India and occupies the highest legal office in the country.

The office is established under Article 76 of the Constitution.

The Attorney General is appointed by the President of India.

To be eligible for appointment, a person must be qualified to be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court.

This means the individual must satisfy one of the constitutional qualifications prescribed for Supreme Court judges, such as being a distinguished jurist or possessing the requisite judicial or advocacy experience.

The Attorney General advises the Union Government on legal matters referred to him and performs legal duties assigned by the President.

He also represents the Government of India in important constitutional and legal proceedings before courts.

Unlike constitutional offices such as the President, Vice-President, or judges of constitutional courts, the Attorney General does not enjoy a fixed tenure.

Instead, he holds office during the pleasure of the President.

In practice, the Attorney General generally serves as long as he enjoys the confidence of the government in power.

By constitutional convention, the Attorney General often resigns when a Council of Ministers leaves office, enabling the incoming government to appoint its own principal legal advisor.

The office occupies a unique position because it combines constitutional status with a close advisory relationship to the executive branch.

AspectProvision
Constitutional ArticleArticle 76
Appointing AuthorityPresident of India
PositionHighest Law Officer of India
QualificationQualified to be a Supreme Court Judge
TenureDuring the Pleasure of the President
UPSC Revision: Article 76 → Attorney General is India's highest law officer. Appointed by the President, must be qualified to become a Supreme Court Judge, and holds office during the President's pleasure.

Rights of Audience and Parliamentary Privileges of the Attorney General

Statement: The Attorney General enjoys the right of audience in all courts of India and may participate in Parliamentary proceedings, but has no voting rights.

The Constitution grants the Attorney General several special privileges to enable effective performance of his duties as the chief legal advisor to the Union Government.

One of the most important among these is the right of audience in all courts within the territory of India.

This means that the Attorney General may appear before any court in the country, including the Supreme Court, High Courts, and subordinate courts, when representing the interests of the Union Government.

This privilege reflects the constitutional importance of the office and ensures that the Government's principal legal officer can intervene wherever necessary.

In addition to judicial privileges, the Attorney General enjoys certain parliamentary rights.

He has the constitutional right to speak and participate in the proceedings of both Houses of Parliament.

He may also participate in the proceedings of any Parliamentary Committee of which he is named a member.

This enables him to explain legal aspects of government policy, clarify constitutional issues, and assist Parliament during debates involving legal or constitutional questions.

However, despite these extensive participatory privileges, the Attorney General is not a Member of Parliament by virtue of his office.

Consequently, he does not possess the right to vote in either House.

This distinction is frequently tested in competitive examinations because candidates often assume that participation automatically includes voting rights.

The Attorney General thus occupies a unique constitutional position—he can participate almost like a member of Parliament but cannot influence decisions through voting.

PrivilegePosition
Right of Audience in CourtsYes, in all courts of India
Can Speak in Lok SabhaYes
Can Speak in Rajya SabhaYes

Member of Parliament?No
UPSC Revision: Attorney General can speak and participate in both Houses of Parliament and has the right of audience in all courts. However, he is not an MP and therefore cannot vote.

Rajya Sabha: The Permanent House of Parliament

Statement: Rajya Sabha is a permanent chamber of Parliament that is never dissolved, with members serving staggered six-year terms.

The Rajya Sabha, also known as the Council of States, is the Upper House of the Indian Parliament.

Unlike the Lok Sabha, it is a permanent body and is not subject to dissolution.

This constitutional design ensures continuity in the legislative process even when the Lok Sabha is dissolved and fresh elections are being conducted.

Members of the Rajya Sabha enjoy a tenure of six years.

However, all members do not retire simultaneously.

To preserve continuity, approximately one-third of the members retire every second year, and elections are held to fill the resulting vacancies.

This system creates a rotating membership structure while ensuring that the House itself remains in existence at all times.

The representatives of states in the Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies (MLAs).

The election is conducted through the system of Proportional Representation by means of the Single Transferable Vote (STV).

This method allows representation of different political groups in proportion to their strength within the State Legislature.

A frequently tested constitutional distinction is that nominated members do not participate in electing Rajya Sabha representatives, and members of State Legislative Councils (MLCs) have no role in this election process.

The Rajya Sabha therefore serves both as a federal chamber representing states and as a stabilizing institution providing continuity to Parliament.

FeatureRajya Sabha
NaturePermanent House
Subject to Dissolution?No
Term of Members6 Years

UPSC Revision: Rajya Sabha is a permanent House and never dissolves. Members serve 6-year terms, with one-third retiring every two years. State representatives are elected by elected MLAs through proportional representation using the Single Transferable Vote system.

Lok Sabha: Composition, Tenure and Dissolution

Statement: The Lok Sabha is the directly elected House of the People and normally continues for five years unless dissolved earlier.

The Lok Sabha, popularly known as the House of the People, is the Lower House of the Indian Parliament.

Unlike the Rajya Sabha, it is not a permanent body.

The normal tenure of the Lok Sabha is five years, calculated from the date appointed for its first meeting after a general election.

At the end of this period, the House automatically stands dissolved unless its term has been extended during a National Emergency as permitted by the Constitution.

The President also possesses the authority to dissolve the Lok Sabha before the completion of its full term, usually on the advice of the Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers.

Dissolution brings the life of the House to an end and necessitates fresh general elections.

The members of the Lok Sabha are chosen through direct elections conducted on the basis of Universal Adult Franchise.

Every Indian citizen who satisfies the prescribed age and electoral qualifications has the right to vote.

The country is divided into territorial constituencies, and each constituency elects one representative to the Lok Sabha.

This system ensures direct democratic representation of the people in the national legislature.

Because the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Lok Sabha, the House occupies a particularly important position in India's parliamentary democracy.

The power to remove a government through a No-Confidence Motion further strengthens its role as the primary representative institution of the people.

FeatureLok Sabha
NatureTemporary House
Normal Tenure5 Years
Subject to Dissolution?Yes
Election MethodDirect Election
Voting BasisUniversal Adult Franchise
ConstituenciesTerritorial Constituencies
UPSC Revision: Lok Sabha is not a permanent House. It normally lasts for 5 years, can be dissolved earlier by the President, and its members are directly elected through Universal Adult Franchise from territorial constituencies.

Speaker of Lok Sabha and Chairman of Rajya Sabha: Roles and Powers

Statement: The Speaker is elected from among Lok Sabha members, whereas the Chairman of Rajya Sabha is the Vice-President of India serving ex-officio. The Speaker also exercises important powers under the Tenth Schedule.

The two Houses of Parliament are presided over by different constitutional authorities.

The Speaker of the Lok Sabha is elected by the members of the Lok Sabha from among themselves.

He serves as the principal presiding officer of the House and is responsible for maintaining order, regulating debates, interpreting rules of procedure, and protecting the privileges of the House.

In contrast, the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha is the Vice-President of India who occupies the office ex-officio under Article 64.

Unlike the Speaker, the Chairman is not elected from among the members of the Rajya Sabha and is not a member of either House of Parliament.

One of the most significant powers of the Speaker relates to the Tenth Schedule, commonly known as the Anti-Defection Law.

The Speaker decides questions regarding the disqualification of Lok Sabha members on grounds of defection.

This authority has major implications for party discipline and legislative stability.

Another important function arises during voting.

The Speaker ordinarily does not vote in the first instance.

However, when votes are equally divided, the Speaker exercises a Casting Vote to break the deadlock.

The Speaker also certifies whether a bill is a Money Bill under Article 110, and such certification carries special constitutional significance.

Thus, while both presiding officers ensure smooth functioning of their respective Houses, the Speaker of the Lok Sabha enjoys several unique constitutional powers that have no direct equivalent in the Rajya Sabha.

Mode of SelectionElected by Lok Sabha MembersVice-President Ex-Officio

Casting VoteYes, in case of tieYes, in case of tie

UPSC Revision: Speaker is elected from Lok Sabha members and decides Anti-Defection cases under the Tenth Schedule. Chairman of Rajya Sabha is the Vice-President ex-officio and is not a member of either House. Speaker also certifies Money Bills.

Sessions of Parliament and Quorum Requirements

Statement: The Constitution requires that the gap between two sessions of Parliament shall not exceed six months, and the quorum for conducting business is one-tenth of the total membership of the House.

The Constitution ensures that Parliament meets regularly so that the executive remains accountable to the legislature.

Under constitutional provisions, the interval between the last sitting of one session and the first sitting of the next session cannot exceed six months.

This requirement ensures that Parliament meets at least twice every year.

In practice, Parliament generally convenes three major sessions: the Budget Session, the Monsoon Session, and the Winter Session.

The responsibility for summoning Parliament formally rests with the President of India, acting on the advice of the Council of Ministers.

Another important procedural requirement is the concept of Quorum.

Quorum refers to the minimum number of members whose presence is necessary for the House to transact business validly.

For both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha, the quorum is fixed at one-tenth of the total number of members of the House.

The presiding officer is included while calculating the quorum.

If at any time the required quorum is not present, the presiding officer may either adjourn the House or suspend the meeting until sufficient members are available.

The quorum rule ensures that parliamentary decisions are not taken by an unrepresentatively small number of members.

Questions relating to session gaps and quorum are frequently asked in UPSC Preliminary examinations because they involve precise constitutional figures.

QuorumOne-Tenth of Total Membership

UPSC Revision: Maximum gap between two parliamentary sessions = 6 months. Quorum in either House = 1/10th of total membership including the presiding officer.

Adjournment, Prorogation and Dissolution: Understanding Parliamentary Terminations

Statement: Adjournment, prorogation and dissolution are three distinct methods of ending parliamentary proceedings, each having different constitutional consequences.

In parliamentary practice, it is essential to distinguish between Adjournment, Prorogation, and Dissolution.

These terms are often confused in examinations, but each refers to a different stage in the functioning of Parliament.

Adjournment refers to the suspension of a sitting of a House for a specified period such as a few hours, a day, several days, or even weeks.

It does not terminate the session itself.

The power of adjournment is exercised directly by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha or the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.

After adjournment, the House meets again as part of the same session.

Prorogation, on the other hand, terminates an entire session of Parliament.

It is carried out by the President of India through a formal notification.

When a session is prorogued, all sittings of that session come to an end.

However, the House itself continues to exist.

Dissolution is the most extensive form of termination and applies only to the Lok Sabha.

Dissolution completely ends the life of the House.

Once dissolved, all members cease to hold their seats and fresh elections become necessary.

Dissolution is also effected by the President through notification.

The Rajya Sabha, being a permanent House, can never be dissolved.

UPSC Revision: Adjournment = ends a sitting (Speaker/Chairman). Prorogation = ends a session (President). Dissolution = ends the life of Lok Sabha (President). Rajya Sabha can never be dissolved.

Question Hour: The Primary Instrument of Executive Accountability

Statement: Question Hour is the first hour of a parliamentary sitting and allows members to seek information and hold the government accountable through questions.

Question Hour is one of the most important parliamentary devices available to members for exercising control over the executive.

It is conducted during the first hour of every parliamentary sitting.

During this period, Members of Parliament ask questions to ministers regarding the policies, decisions, administration, and functioning of the government.

The device serves as a vital mechanism of transparency and accountability in a parliamentary democracy.

Questions asked during Question Hour are broadly classified into Starred Questions and Unstarred Questions.

A Starred Question requires an oral answer from the concerned minister on the floor of the House.

Because the answer is delivered orally, members are permitted to ask supplementary questions for further clarification.

This often leads to detailed exchanges and enables members to scrutinize governmental actions more effectively.

An Unstarred Question, in contrast, requires a written answer.

The reply is laid on the table of the House and becomes part of the official parliamentary record.

Since the answer is written, no supplementary questions are allowed.

This distinction between oral and written answers is a favorite area for UPSC prelims questions.

Question Hour thus remains one of the most visible and effective instruments through which Parliament ensures ministerial accountability.

FeatureStarred QuestionUnstarred Question

UPSC Revision: Question Hour = First hour of a parliamentary sitting. Starred Questions receive oral answers and allow supplementary questions. Unstarred Questions receive written answers and do not permit supplementary questions.

No-Confidence Motion: Parliamentary Test of Majority Support

Statement: A No-Confidence Motion can be moved only in the Lok Sabha and, if passed, compels the entire Council of Ministers to resign.

The No-Confidence Motion is one of the most powerful instruments available to the Lok Sabha for controlling the executive.

It is based on the constitutional principle that the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

Through this motion, members of the House formally express that they no longer have confidence in the government.

A No-Confidence Motion can be introduced and moved only in the Lok Sabha.

The Rajya Sabha has no authority to initiate or pass such a motion because the government is not collectively responsible to it.

Before the motion is admitted for discussion, it must secure the support of at least 50 members of the Lok Sabha.

This requirement prevents frivolous or insignificant motions from consuming parliamentary time.

Once admitted, the motion is debated in the House and put to vote.

If a simple majority of members present and voting support the motion, the government is deemed to have lost the confidence of the House.

In such a situation, the entire Council of Ministers must resign collectively.

The motion is directed against the government as a whole rather than against an individual minister.

Its success signifies that the executive no longer enjoys majority support in the popularly elected House and therefore cannot legitimately continue in office.

The No-Confidence Motion is thus a central feature of India's parliamentary system and an essential mechanism for ensuring executive accountability.

AspectProvision
Can Be Moved InLok Sabha Only

Voting RequirementSimple Majority
Effect if PassedEntire Ministry Resigns
UPSC Revision: No-Confidence Motion can be moved only in Lok Sabha. It requires support of 50 members for admission and, if passed by a simple majority, the entire Council of Ministers must resign.

Censure Motion vs No-Confidence Motion

Statement: A Censure Motion differs from a No-Confidence Motion because it must specify reasons and does not necessarily require the resignation of the government if passed.

Both the Censure Motion and the No-Confidence Motion are parliamentary devices used to criticize the government, but they differ significantly in their purpose, scope, and consequences.

A No-Confidence Motion is directed against the Council of Ministers as a whole and seeks to determine whether the government continues to enjoy the confidence of the Lok Sabha.

It does not require the mover to state specific reasons for bringing the motion.

If passed, the government must resign collectively because it has lost majority support in the House.

A Censure Motion, on the other hand, is moved to express strong disapproval of a particular policy, action, decision, or conduct of the government or a minister.

Unlike a No-Confidence Motion, it must clearly specify the grounds or reasons on which it is based.

The motion may be directed against an individual minister, a group of ministers, or even the entire Council of Ministers.

Its objective is primarily to criticize and hold the government accountable rather than to test its majority.

Another major distinction concerns the consequences of passage.

Even if a Censure Motion is passed, the government is not constitutionally required to resign.

However, its passage may carry serious political implications and indicate dissatisfaction within the House.

Thus, while both motions are tools of parliamentary control, only the No-Confidence Motion directly determines whether a government can continue in office.

PurposeTest Majority SupportCriticize Policy/Action
Reasons Required?NoYes
Against Whom?Entire MinistryMinister or Entire Ministry
Effect if PassedGovernment Must ResignNo Mandatory Resignation
UPSC Revision: No-Confidence Motion tests majority support, requires no specific reasons, and if passed the government must resign. Censure Motion requires specific grounds, may target an individual minister, and its passage does not mandate resignation.

Money Bills under Article 110: Introduction and Certification

Statement: A Money Bill can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha with the prior recommendation of the President, and the Speaker's certification is final.

A Money Bill is a special category of legislation defined under Article 110 of the Constitution of India.

It deals exclusively with matters such as taxation, borrowing of money by the government, custody of the Consolidated Fund of India, appropriation of money, and other financial matters specifically mentioned in Article 110.

The Constitution grants a unique position to Money Bills because they directly concern the financial powers of the government.

One of the most important constitutional requirements is that a Money Bill can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha.

The Rajya Sabha has no authority to originate a Money Bill.

Furthermore, introduction of a Money Bill requires the prior recommendation of the President.

This requirement ensures executive approval before Parliament considers legislation involving core financial matters.

Another crucial feature relates to certification.

The Speaker of the Lok Sabha has the authority to certify whether a bill is a Money Bill.

This certification carries enormous constitutional significance because it determines the legislative procedure that the bill will subsequently follow.

According to constitutional practice, the Speaker's decision regarding certification is regarded as final.

As a result, questions concerning whether a bill qualifies as a Money Bill are closely associated with the constitutional authority of the Speaker.

For UPSC examinations, candidates must remember the combination of three elements: exclusive introduction in the Lok Sabha, prior presidential recommendation, and certification by the Speaker.

FeatureMoney Bill
Constitutional ArticleArticle 110
Can Be Introduced InLok Sabha Only
Presidential Recommendation Required?Yes
Certification AuthoritySpeaker of Lok Sabha
Speaker's DecisionFinal
UPSC Revision: Money Bill (Article 110) can originate only in Lok Sabha, requires prior recommendation of the President, and must be certified by the Speaker whose decision is treated as final.

Rajya Sabha's Limited Role in Money Bills: The 14-Day Rule

Statement: The Rajya Sabha cannot reject or amend a Money Bill and must return it within 14 days with or without recommendations.

The Constitution grants the Lok Sabha a dominant position in matters relating to Money Bills.

Once a Money Bill is passed by the Lok Sabha, it is transmitted to the Rajya Sabha for its recommendations.

However, unlike ordinary legislation, the powers of the Rajya Sabha over a Money Bill are severely restricted.

The Rajya Sabha cannot reject a Money Bill.

It also cannot amend the bill on its own authority.

Instead, it may only suggest recommendations for consideration by the Lok Sabha.

The Constitution requires the Rajya Sabha to return the bill within a period of 14 days from the date of its receipt.

The bill may be returned either with recommendations or without any recommendations.

If the Lok Sabha accepts some or all of the recommendations, the bill proceeds in the modified form.

However, the Lok Sabha is completely free to reject every recommendation made by the Rajya Sabha.

If it chooses to do so, the bill is deemed to have been passed in the form originally approved by the Lok Sabha.

An even more significant provision applies when the Rajya Sabha fails to return the bill within the stipulated period of fourteen days.

In such a case, the Money Bill is automatically deemed to have been passed by both Houses at the expiry of the fourteen-day period.

This arrangement reflects the constitutional principle that financial control primarily belongs to the directly elected House of the People.

AspectRajya Sabha's Position
Can Introduce Money Bill?No
Can Reject Money Bill?No
Can Amend Money Bill?No
Can Recommend Changes?Yes
Time Limit14 Days
Lok Sabha Bound by Recommendations?No
UPSC Revision: Rajya Sabha cannot reject or amend a Money Bill. It must return the bill within 14 days with or without recommendations, and Lok Sabha may accept or reject all recommendations. Failure to return within 14 days results in deemed passage.

Financial Bills under Article 117: Distinction from Money Bills

Statement: Financial Bills are broader than Money Bills and are classified into Financial Bill (I) and Financial Bill (II), with different procedural requirements.

Although Money Bills and Financial Bills both deal with financial matters, they are not identical constitutional categories.

Article 117 classifies Financial Bills into two types: Financial Bill (I) and Financial Bill (II).

A clear understanding of this distinction is important because UPSC frequently asks questions comparing these bills.

Financial Bill (I) contains one or more matters mentioned in Article 110, along with other provisions that are not strictly related to Money Bill subjects.

Because it includes Article 110 matters, it can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha and requires the prior recommendation of the President.

However, unlike a Money Bill, Financial Bill (I) does not enjoy special protection from the Rajya Sabha.

The Rajya Sabha can discuss, amend, reject, or delay it just as it can an ordinary bill.

In the event of disagreement between the two Houses, a Joint Sitting may be summoned.

Financial Bill (II) is different because it does not contain any matter specified in Article 110.

It merely involves expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India or other financial implications.

Such a bill can be introduced in either House of Parliament, although presidential recommendation is required before consideration and passage.

Thus, every Money Bill is a financial bill, but every Financial Bill is not a Money Bill.

The key distinction lies in the scope of content and the powers available to the Rajya Sabha.

FeatureMoney BillFinancial Bill (I)Financial Bill (II)
Article110117(1)117(3)
Contains Article 110 MattersExclusivelyYes + Other MattersNo
Can Originate in Rajya Sabha?NoNoYes
Presidential RecommendationRequiredRequiredRequired before consideration
Rajya Sabha PowersRecommendations OnlyFull Legislative PowersFull Legislative Powers
Joint Sitting Possible?NoYesYes
UPSC Revision: Financial Bill (I) contains Article 110 matters plus other provisions and behaves like an ordinary bill in Rajya Sabha. Financial Bill (II) contains no Article 110 matter and may originate in either House. Every Money Bill is a Financial Bill, but not vice versa.

Joint Sitting of Parliament (Article 108)

Statement: A Joint Sitting is summoned by the President to resolve legislative deadlocks between the two Houses and is presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.

The Constitution provides a mechanism known as a Joint Sitting of Parliament under Article 108 to resolve legislative disagreements between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha.

A Joint Sitting may be summoned only when a deadlock arises regarding an Ordinary Bill or a Financial Bill.

Deadlock situations generally occur when one House rejects a bill passed by the other House, when the Houses finally disagree on amendments, or when one House delays a bill for an extended period.

The authority to summon a Joint Sitting rests exclusively with the President of India.

However, this mechanism is not available for every type of legislation.

Money Bills and Constitutional Amendment Bills are specifically excluded from the Joint Sitting procedure.

This exclusion is frequently tested in UPSC examinations.

The Joint Sitting is normally presided over by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.

If the Speaker is absent, the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides.

If both are absent, the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha presides over the sitting.

An important constitutional point is that the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (Vice-President of India) cannot preside over a Joint Sitting.

This is because he is not a member of either House of Parliament.

Once convened, members of both Houses sit and vote together, and the bill is decided by a majority of members present and voting.

Since the Lok Sabha generally has a larger membership, its numerical strength often influences the outcome of Joint Sittings.

AspectProvision
Constitutional ArticleArticle 108
Summoned ByPresident of India
Applicable ToOrdinary Bills & Financial Bills
Not Applicable ToMoney Bills & Constitutional Amendment Bills
Presiding OfficerSpeaker of Lok Sabha
If Speaker AbsentDeputy Speaker
If Both AbsentDeputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha
UPSC Revision: Joint Sitting (Article 108) is summoned by the President to resolve deadlocks over Ordinary and Financial Bills. It is presided over by the Speaker of Lok Sabha and is not available for Money Bills or Constitutional Amendment Bills.

Lapsing of Bills on Dissolution of the Lok Sabha

Statement: The dissolution of the Lok Sabha affects different categories of pending bills differently, making the rules of lapsing a highly important constitutional topic.

The dissolution of the Lok Sabha does not affect all pending bills in the same manner.

The Constitution and parliamentary practice distinguish between various stages of legislative progress to determine whether a bill lapses or survives.

This topic is considered a classic UPSC prelims trap because several categories appear similar but produce different outcomes.

The first rule is straightforward: a bill pending in the Lok Sabha at the time of dissolution automatically lapses.

Since the House itself ceases to exist, all unfinished legislative business before it comes to an end.

The second rule states that a bill passed by the Lok Sabha but pending in the Rajya Sabha also lapses upon dissolution.

This occurs because the originating House has ceased to exist before the legislative process could be completed.

The third rule is a major exception and frequently appears in examinations.

A bill pending in the Rajya Sabha but not yet passed by the Lok Sabha does not lapse.

The reason is that the Rajya Sabha is a permanent House and continues to function even after the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.

The fourth rule concerns bills that have already completed the parliamentary stage.

A bill passed by both Houses but awaiting Presidential assent does not lapse.

Such a bill remains valid and may receive assent even after the Lok Sabha has been dissolved.

Remembering these distinctions is essential because UPSC often frames questions by slightly altering the stage at which the bill is pending.

Status of Bill at DissolutionLapses?
Pending in Lok SabhaYes
Passed by Lok Sabha, Pending in Rajya SabhaYes
Pending in Rajya Sabha, Not Passed by Lok SabhaNo
Passed by Both Houses, Awaiting Presidential AssentNo
Exam Trap: A bill pending only in the Rajya Sabha does NOT lapse on dissolution of the Lok Sabha because the Rajya Sabha is a permanent House.
UPSC Revision: Bills pending in Lok Sabha or passed by Lok Sabha but pending in Rajya Sabha lapse. Bills pending only in Rajya Sabha and bills awaiting Presidential assent after passage by both Houses do not lapse.

Annual Financial Statement (Budget) – Article 112

Statement: The Constitution uses the term 'Annual Financial Statement' instead of 'Budget' and classifies expenditure into charged and votable categories.

The yearly financial plan of the Government of India is constitutionally known as the Annual Financial Statement (AFS) under Article 112.

An important constitutional fact is that the word 'Budget' does not appear anywhere in the text of the Constitution.

The term 'Budget' is merely a popular and conventional expression used in parliamentary practice.

The Annual Financial Statement presents the estimated receipts and expenditures of the Government of India for the upcoming financial year.

It is laid before both Houses of Parliament by the President through the Finance Minister.

One of the most important distinctions made in the Budget is between Charged Expenditure and Voted Expenditure.

Charged Expenditure refers to expenditure charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India.

Such expenditure is not submitted to the vote of Parliament, although it may be discussed.

Examples include the salary and allowances of the President of India, judges of the Supreme Court, the Comptroller and Auditor General of India, and certain debt charges.

Voted Expenditure, on the other hand, consists of expenditure that must receive parliamentary approval through the process of demands for grants.

The Lok Sabha has the authority to discuss, reduce, or approve these demands.

This distinction is intended to protect the independence of important constitutional offices while maintaining legislative control over most government spending.

FeatureCharged ExpenditureVoted Expenditure
Parliamentary Vote Required?NoYes
Can Be Discussed?YesYes
ExamplesPresident, SC Judges, CAG SalariesGovernment Department Expenditure
Key FactProvision
Constitutional TermAnnual Financial Statement
Article112
Word 'Budget' in Constitution?No
UPSC Revision: Budget is constitutionally called the Annual Financial Statement (Article 112). Charged expenditure is not votable by Parliament, whereas voted expenditure requires approval through demands for grants.

Cut Motions: Parliamentary Control over Government Expenditure

Statement: Cut Motions are devices available to Lok Sabha members to oppose or reduce demands for grants presented by the government.

After the Budget is presented, the expenditure proposals of various ministries are submitted to the Lok Sabha in the form of Demands for Grants.

Members of the Lok Sabha can move Cut Motions to express dissatisfaction with government policies, highlight wasteful expenditure, or draw attention to specific grievances.

These motions are important instruments of financial control exercised by Parliament over the executive.

There are three types of Cut Motions recognized in parliamentary practice.

The first is the Policy Cut Motion.

Under this motion, the amount of a demand is reduced to ₹1.

This symbolic reduction signifies complete disapproval of the policy underlying the demand and provides an opportunity for a broad discussion on government policy.

The second type is the Economy Cut Motion.

In this case, the demand is reduced by a specified amount.

The objective is to suggest that the proposed expenditure can be reduced without affecting essential functions.

The third type is the Token Cut Motion.

Here, the demand is reduced by ₹100.

This reduction is symbolic and is intended to draw attention to a specific grievance within the sphere of responsibility of the government.

Although Cut Motions are rarely passed in practice because governments generally enjoy majority support in the Lok Sabha, they remain valuable tools for debate, accountability, and legislative scrutiny.

Type of Cut MotionReductionPurpose
Policy CutTo ₹1Complete Disapproval of Policy
Economy CutSpecified AmountEconomy in Expenditure
Token Cut₹100Highlight Specific Grievance
Exam Trap: Policy Cut = ₹1, Token Cut = ₹100. UPSC frequently asks these exact figures.
UPSC Revision: Three Cut Motions: Policy Cut (reduce to ₹1), Economy Cut (reduce by a specified amount), and Token Cut (reduce by ₹100). They are used by Lok Sabha members to scrutinize government expenditure.

Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266)

Statement: The Consolidated Fund of India is the principal account of the Government of India, and no money can be withdrawn from it without parliamentary authorization.

The Consolidated Fund of India (CFI) is established under Article 266(1) of the Constitution and is regarded as the most important government fund.

It functions as the chief treasury account into which virtually all revenues and receipts of the Union Government are deposited.

All tax revenues collected by the Government of India, such as income tax, customs duties, and GST receipts belonging to the Union, are credited to this fund.

In addition, all loans raised by the government, treasury bills issued, and recoveries of loans previously granted by the government are also deposited into the Consolidated Fund.

Because it contains the primary financial resources of the Union, strict constitutional safeguards govern withdrawals from this fund.

No amount can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund of India unless Parliament authorizes such expenditure.

This authorization is provided through the passage of an Appropriation Act after the demands for grants and budgetary proposals have been approved.

The requirement of parliamentary approval ensures legislative control over public finances and prevents unauthorized spending by the executive.

Most government expenditure, including administrative expenses, developmental schemes, defence spending, and welfare programs, is ultimately met from the Consolidated Fund.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that all ordinary government revenues flow into this fund and that withdrawal requires parliamentary sanction.

FeatureConsolidated Fund of India
Constitutional ArticleArticle 266(1)
NatureMain Treasury of Government of India
ReceivesTaxes, Loans, Loan Recoveries
Withdrawal Allowed ThroughAppropriation Act
Parliamentary Approval Required?Yes
Exam Trap: Both charged and voted expenditures are drawn from the Consolidated Fund, but only voted expenditure requires parliamentary voting. Withdrawal in all cases occurs through an Appropriation Act.
UPSC Revision: Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266) is the main account of the Union Government. All revenues, loans, and loan recoveries are deposited here, and no money can be withdrawn without parliamentary authorization through an Appropriation Act.

Contingency Fund of India (Article 267)

Statement: The Contingency Fund of India exists to meet unforeseen expenditures and is placed at the disposal of the President of India.

The Contingency Fund of India is established under Article 267 of the Constitution to provide financial flexibility in situations requiring immediate expenditure.

Unlike the Consolidated Fund, which operates through detailed parliamentary authorization, the Contingency Fund is designed for emergencies and unforeseen circumstances where waiting for parliamentary approval may not be practical.

The fund is placed at the disposal of the President of India.

This means that advances can be made from the fund to meet urgent and unexpected expenses pending subsequent approval by Parliament.

Examples may include emergency relief measures following natural disasters, urgent security-related expenditures, or other unforeseen governmental obligations.

Although constitutionally vested in the President, the fund is administered on behalf of the President by the executive machinery of the government.

In practice, it is operated by the Finance Secretary under the authority of the Union Government.

It is important to understand that expenditure from the Contingency Fund is only a temporary advance.

After the expenditure is incurred, the government must seek approval from Parliament.

Once Parliament authorizes the expenditure, the amount withdrawn is recouped by transferring an equivalent amount from the Consolidated Fund of India back into the Contingency Fund.

Thus, the fund acts as a temporary financial buffer rather than a permanent source of expenditure.

For UPSC examinations, the most important facts are its constitutional basis under Article 267, its emergency purpose, and its placement at the disposal of the President.

FeatureContingency Fund of India
Constitutional ArticleArticle 267
PurposeUnforeseen & Emergency Expenditure
Placed at Disposal OfPresident of India
Operated ByFinance Secretary on behalf of President
Parliamentary Approval Needed Later?Yes
Exam Trap: The Contingency Fund is at the disposal of the President, but expenditure is only temporary. Parliamentary approval must be obtained later and the fund is subsequently replenished from the Consolidated Fund.
UPSC Revision: Contingency Fund of India (Article 267) is meant for urgent and unforeseen expenditure. It is placed at the disposal of the President, operated by the Finance Secretary, and later requires parliamentary approval for recoupment.

Public Account of India (Article 266(2))

Statement: The Public Account of India contains money held by the government in trust and does not require parliamentary authorization for withdrawals.

The Public Account of India is established under Article 266(2) of the Constitution and serves a purpose distinct from both the Consolidated Fund and the Contingency Fund.

This account contains funds that do not belong to the Government of India in the strict sense but are held by it as a banker, trustee, or custodian.

Examples include Provident Fund deposits, Small Savings collections, Public Provident Fund (PPF) deposits, postal savings, judicial deposits, and other similar liabilities.

Since these amounts belong to individuals, institutions, or specific entities and are merely managed by the government, they are kept separately from ordinary government revenues.

The most important constitutional distinction concerns withdrawals.

Unlike the Consolidated Fund of India, money can be withdrawn from the Public Account without parliamentary authorization.

No Appropriation Act is required for such withdrawals.

The executive government may operate this account directly according to established rules and procedures.

This arrangement exists because the government is essentially returning or managing money that it holds in trust rather than spending its own revenue.

Consequently, legislative approval is not necessary each time funds are withdrawn.

UPSC frequently frames comparative questions involving the three constitutional funds, making it essential to remember that the Public Account alone operates without parliamentary voting or appropriation.

A useful memory aid is that the Consolidated Fund contains the government's money, the Contingency Fund handles emergency advances, while the Public Account contains money held on behalf of others.

Parliamentary Authorization Required?No
