Exam Trap: Public Account withdrawals do not require an Appropriation Act because the money does not belong to the government; it is merely held in trust.
UPSC Revision: Public Account of India (Article 266(2)) contains funds like provident funds, small savings, and deposits held in trust by the government. Withdrawals are made through executive action and do not require parliamentary authorization.

Governor: Dual Role as Constitutional Head and Central Agent

Statement: The Governor performs a dual constitutional role by serving both as the nominal executive head of the state and as an agent of the Union Government.

The office of the Governor occupies a unique position within India's federal structure.

Like the President at the Union level, the Governor serves as the constitutional or nominal head of the state government.

All executive actions of the state are formally carried out in the Governor's name.

However, in actual practice, these powers are exercised on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Chief Minister.

In addition to being the constitutional head of the state, the Governor also functions as an agent of the Central Government.

This aspect distinguishes the Governor from many other constitutional authorities.

As the representative of the Union in the state, the Governor plays an important role in maintaining coordination between the Centre and the States.

The Governor may report constitutional breakdowns, monitor compliance with constitutional provisions, and discharge functions assigned by the Constitution or Parliament.

An important constitutional development occurred through the 7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956.

Before this amendment, each state generally had its own Governor.

The amendment made it possible for the same individual to be appointed as Governor of two or more states simultaneously.

This provision continues to be used in practice, particularly when administrative convenience or political considerations require such arrangements.

The dual nature of the Governor's office is frequently tested in UPSC examinations because it reflects the balance between federal autonomy and national unity.

RoleFunction
Constitutional HeadNominal Executive of the State
Central AgentRepresents Union Government in the State
Key FactProvision
One Governor for Multiple States7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956
UPSC Revision: Governor has a dual role—constitutional head of the state and agent of the Central Government. The 7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956 allows the same person to serve as Governor of two or more states.

Governor: Appointment and Pleasure of the President

Statement: The Governor is appointed by the President and holds office during the pleasure of the President without any guaranteed constitutional tenure.

The Governor of a state is not elected either directly by the people or indirectly by an electoral college.

Instead, the Constitution provides for appointment by the President of India.

The appointment is made through a warrant under the President's hand and seal.

This method was adopted by the Constitution-makers to ensure a constitutional link between the Union and the States.

Although the Constitution prescribes a nominal term of five years for the Governor, this does not provide absolute security of tenure.

The Governor holds office during the pleasure of the President.

This means the President may remove a Governor before the completion of the five-year term.

No impeachment procedure, legislative resolution, or judicial inquiry is required for such removal.

Unlike judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts, Governors do not enjoy constitutional protection against removal.

The Constitution does not specify any grounds upon which a Governor may be removed.

Consequently, the Governor's continuation in office depends upon the confidence of the Union executive acting through the President.

The Governor may also resign by submitting a resignation letter addressed to the President.

This arrangement reflects the Governor's unique constitutional position as both the head of the state executive and a representative of the Union.

For UPSC examinations, the phrase 'holds office during the pleasure of the President' is one of the most important facts associated with the Governor's office.

FeatureGovernor
Appointed ByPresident of India
MethodWarrant under Hand and Seal
Nominal Term5 Years
Security of TenureNo
Removal PrinciplePleasure of the President
Exam Trap: Governor has a five-year term but does not have a guaranteed tenure. The office is held during the pleasure of the President, who may remove the Governor before completion of the term.
UPSC Revision: Governor is appointed by the President and holds office during the pleasure of the President. Unlike judges, Governors have no constitutional security of tenure and may be removed without specified grounds.

Governor's Discretionary Powers under Article 163

Statement: The Governor enjoys wider constitutional discretion than the President and may act independently in certain matters specified by the Constitution.

The Governor normally acts on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Chief Minister.

However, unlike the President of India, the Governor possesses certain constitutional discretionary powers.

These powers arise from the special wording of Article 163, which recognizes situations where the Governor may act in his or her discretion.

This makes the Governor's office unique within India's constitutional framework.

The President generally functions on ministerial advice except in a few limited circumstances.

By contrast, the Governor may exercise discretion in specific constitutional situations, such as inviting a party to form the government when no clear majority exists, recommending President's Rule, or performing special responsibilities assigned by the Constitution.

Article 163 contains an important constitutional safeguard regarding these discretionary powers.

If any question arises as to whether a particular matter falls within the Governor's discretion, the decision of the Governor is final.

The validity of actions taken on such grounds cannot be challenged merely because the Governor claimed to be acting in discretion.

This provision was intended to prevent constant legal disputes over the scope of discretionary authority.

Nevertheless, modern constitutional practice and judicial interpretation have emphasized that discretionary powers should be exercised carefully and within constitutional limits.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that the Governor's discretionary powers are broader than those of the President and are explicitly recognized in the Constitution.

AspectGovernor
Normal RuleActs on Aid and Advice of Council of Ministers
Discretionary PowersExplicitly Recognized under Article 163
Decision on Whether Matter is DiscretionaryGovernor's Decision is Final
Exam Trap: Unlike the President, the Governor enjoys constitutionally recognized discretionary powers. Article 163 specifically provides that the Governor's decision regarding discretionary matters is final.
UPSC Revision: Article 163 grants the Governor explicit discretionary powers. If a dispute arises over whether a matter falls within discretion, the Governor's decision is constitutionally treated as final.

Governor's Veto Powers and Reservation of Bills (Article 200)

Statement: When a bill passed by the State Legislature is presented to the Governor, the Governor may assent, withhold assent, return the bill, or reserve it for the President's consideration.

Under Article 200, every bill passed by the State Legislature is presented to the Governor for consideration before it can become law.

The Constitution grants the Governor several options at this stage.

The Governor may give assent to the bill, in which case it becomes an Act.

The Governor may also withhold assent, effectively exercising an absolute veto in certain circumstances.

A third option is to return the bill for reconsideration by the State Legislature.

This power applies only to ordinary bills and not to Money Bills.

If the Legislature passes the bill again and returns it to the Governor, the Governor is normally expected to grant assent.

The fourth and most significant option is to reserve the bill for the consideration of the President.

Once a bill is reserved, the final decision shifts from the Governor to the President of India.

This mechanism reflects the federal relationship between the Union and the States.

In certain situations, reservation is not merely discretionary but mandatory.

The Governor must reserve a bill if, in the Governor's opinion, it would endanger the constitutional position or powers of the High Court of the state.

This safeguard protects judicial independence and the constitutional status of High Courts.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember all four options available to the Governor and the special mandatory reservation requirement relating to the High Court.

Governor's Options Under Article 200Effect
Give AssentBill Becomes Law
Withhold AssentBill Does Not Become Law
Return for ReconsiderationLegislature Reconsiders the Bill
Reserve for PresidentFinal Decision Taken by President
Exam Trap: The Governor must reserve a bill for the President if it threatens the constitutional position of the State High Court. This is a mandatory reservation, not a discretionary choice.
UPSC Revision: Under Article 200, the Governor may assent, withhold assent, return a bill for reconsideration, or reserve it for the President. Bills affecting the constitutional position of the High Court must be reserved for Presidential consideration.

Governor's Ordinance-Making Power (Article 213)

Statement: The Governor can promulgate ordinances when the State Legislature is not in session, but such ordinances are temporary laws subject to legislative approval.

The Constitution empowers the Governor to issue Ordinances under Article 213 when immediate legislative action is necessary and the State Legislature is not in session.

An ordinance has the same force and effect as an Act passed by the State Legislature.

However, it is only a temporary law-making mechanism designed to meet urgent situations when the legislature cannot immediately assemble.

The Governor may exercise this power only when the Legislative Assembly is not in session.

In states having a bicameral legislature, the ordinance power may be exercised when both Houses are not in session.

Although formally issued by the Governor, the ordinance is ordinarily promulgated on the advice of the Council of Ministers headed by the Chief Minister.

Once the legislature reassembles, the ordinance must be laid before it for approval.

If the ordinance is not approved, it ceases to operate.

Constitutionally, an ordinance remains valid for a maximum period of six weeks from the reassembly of the legislature.

If both Houses are present in a bicameral state, the six-week period is calculated from the date of reassembly of the later House.

The Governor may also withdraw an ordinance at any time before its expiry.

An important limitation distinguishes the Governor's power from ordinary state legislative authority.

The Governor cannot promulgate certain categories of ordinances without obtaining prior instructions from the President, especially in matters where a bill would have required Presidential consideration.

For UPSC examinations, the six-week approval rule and the requirement of prior Presidential instructions in specified cases are particularly important.

FeatureGovernor's Ordinance
Constitutional ArticleArticle 213
When Issued?When State Legislature is Not in Session
Force of LawSame as an Act of Legislature
Maximum Validity After Reassembly6 Weeks
Can Require Presidential Instructions?Yes
Exam Trap: An ordinance does not automatically remain valid for six months. It must be approved within six weeks of the legislature's reassembly; otherwise, it ceases to operate.
UPSC Revision: Under Article 213, the Governor can promulgate ordinances when the State Legislature is not in session. Such ordinances have the force of law but must be approved within six weeks of legislative reassembly and may require prior Presidential instructions in specified cases.

Chief Minister: The Real Executive Head of the State

Statement: While the Governor is the nominal executive head of the state, the Chief Minister is the real executive authority who leads the state government.

India follows the parliamentary system of government at both the Union and State levels.

Accordingly, a distinction exists between the nominal executive and the real executive.

At the state level, the Governor serves as the constitutional or nominal executive head, often described as the De Jure executive.

In contrast, the Chief Minister is the real executive authority, commonly referred to as the De Facto executive.

The Chief Minister heads the Council of Ministers and exercises actual control over the administration of the state.

All major policy decisions, legislative priorities, and executive actions are effectively guided by the Chief Minister and the Council of Ministers.

The Governor generally acts on the aid and advice of this Council.

The Chief Minister also serves as the principal channel of communication between the Governor and the Council of Ministers.

One of the Chief Minister's important constitutional responsibilities is to keep the Governor informed about the affairs of the state government.

The Chief Minister advises the Governor regarding the appointment and removal of ministers and coordinates the functioning of the entire executive machinery.

Because of this central role, the Chief Minister is often described as the pivot around which the state administration revolves.

Understanding the distinction between the Governor's formal powers and the Chief Minister's actual authority is essential for understanding the functioning of state governments.

PositionStatusRole
GovernorDe Jure ExecutiveNominal Constitutional Head
Chief MinisterDe Facto ExecutiveReal Executive Head
Exam Trap: Executive power is formally vested in the Governor, but actual executive authority is exercised by the Chief Minister and Council of Ministers.
UPSC Revision: The Governor is the nominal (De Jure) executive head of the state, while the Chief Minister is the real (De Facto) executive head who leads the Council of Ministers and directs state administration.

Collective Responsibility of the State Council of Ministers

Statement: The State Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly and must resign if it loses the confidence of the House.

The principle of collective responsibility is one of the foundational features of the parliamentary system of government.

At the state level, the Constitution provides that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha).

This means that the Council functions as a unified body and stands or falls together.

Every minister is expected to publicly support decisions taken by the Council of Ministers, regardless of personal opinions expressed during internal discussions.

If the Legislative Assembly passes a No-Confidence Motion against the government, the entire Council of Ministers must resign.

The resignation is not limited to the Chief Minister alone.

All ministers, irrespective of rank, must vacate office because the Council is treated as a single constitutional unit.

This principle ensures that the executive remains accountable to the elected representatives of the people.

An important constitutional point is that collective responsibility exists only toward the Legislative Assembly and not toward the Legislative Council.

Even in states with bicameral legislatures, the Legislative Council cannot determine the survival of the government.

If a minister happens to be a member of the Legislative Council, that minister must also resign if the government loses the confidence of the Legislative Assembly.

The Assembly therefore occupies a superior constitutional position in matters concerning executive accountability.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that collective responsibility is directed exclusively toward the Vidhan Sabha and not the entire legislature.

AspectProvision
Responsible ToLegislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha)
Effect of No-Confidence MotionEntire Ministry Must Resign
Applies to Ministers in Legislative Council?Yes
Responsible to Legislative Council?No
Exam Trap: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Legislative Assembly, not to the Legislative Council or the state legislature as a whole.
UPSC Revision: Collective responsibility means the entire State Council of Ministers is accountable to the Legislative Assembly. If a No-Confidence Motion succeeds, the whole ministry must resign, including ministers belonging to the Legislative Council.

Size of the State Council of Ministers: 15% Ceiling and 12-Member Minimum

Statement: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 imposed limits on the size of the State Council of Ministers to prevent oversized ministries.

For many years, there was no constitutional restriction on the size of the Council of Ministers in the states.

This often resulted in the formation of excessively large ministries, creating concerns about administrative efficiency and political patronage.

To address this issue, Parliament enacted the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003.

The amendment introduced a constitutional ceiling on the number of ministers in state governments.

According to the amended provisions, the total number of ministers, including the Chief Minister, shall not exceed 15% of the total strength of the Legislative Assembly.

This rule applies uniformly across all states.

However, applying the 15% formula to smaller states could result in very small ministries that may not be administratively practical.

To solve this problem, the Constitution also prescribes a minimum threshold.

The total number of ministers in a state government shall not be less than 12.

This ensures that even states with small assemblies can maintain a functional executive structure.

The dual formula therefore combines a maximum limit with a minimum safeguard.

The amendment was part of a broader effort to strengthen political accountability and reduce unnecessary expansion of ministerial offices.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember both numerical limits together: the 15% ceiling and the 12-member floor.

ProvisionRule
Maximum Size of Ministry15% of Total Strength of Legislative Assembly
Minimum Size of Ministry12 Members
Included in CountChief Minister + All Ministers
Inserted By91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003
Exam Trap: The 15% limit includes the Chief Minister. Also remember that the Constitution separately guarantees a minimum ministry size of 12 members.
UPSC Revision: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 limits the size of the State Council of Ministers to a maximum of 15% of the Legislative Assembly's strength, with a minimum requirement of 12 ministers including the Chief Minister.

Legislative Assembly: Composition and Membership Limits

Statement: The Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) is the popularly elected house of the state legislature, with constitutional limits on its membership strength.

The Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) is the lower house of the state legislature and represents the people of the state directly.

Its members are elected through direct elections based on the principle of universal adult suffrage.

Each state is divided into territorial constituencies, and one representative is elected from each constituency.

The Constitution prescribes limits on the size of the Legislative Assembly to ensure both representation and administrative efficiency.

Ordinarily, the total strength of a Legislative Assembly cannot exceed 500 members.

At the same time, the Constitution provides that the strength should not ordinarily be less than 60 members.

These limits create a broad framework within which state legislatures are structured.

However, strict adherence to the minimum requirement would create difficulties for geographically small or less populous states.

Therefore, Parliament has provided special statutory exceptions for certain smaller states.

States such as Sikkim, Goa, and Mizoram have Legislative Assemblies with membership below the normal constitutional minimum of sixty.

This flexibility allows representation to be tailored according to the demographic and administrative realities of individual states.

The Legislative Assembly is also the more powerful chamber in states that possess a bicameral legislature because the executive is collectively responsible to it.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember the membership range of 60–500 and the existence of special exceptions for smaller states.

FeatureLegislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha)
Method of ElectionDirect Election
BasisUniversal Adult Suffrage
Maximum Strength500 Members
Minimum Strength60 Members
Exception StatesReason
Sikkim, Goa, MizoramSpecial Statutory Exceptions to Minimum Strength Rule
Exam Trap: The minimum strength of 60 members is not absolute. Smaller states such as Goa, Sikkim, and Mizoram enjoy statutory exceptions.
UPSC Revision: The Legislative Assembly is directly elected by the people. Its strength normally ranges from 60 to 500 members, though certain smaller states have been granted exceptions below the 60-member minimum.

Creation and Abolition of Legislative Councils (Article 169)

Statement: Parliament may create or abolish a State Legislative Council, but only after receiving a special majority resolution from the concerned State Legislative Assembly.

Not all Indian states have a bicameral legislature.

Some states have only a Legislative Assembly, while others have both a Legislative Assembly and a Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad).

The Constitution provides a flexible mechanism for creating or abolishing Legislative Councils through Article 169.

The process begins in the State Legislative Assembly.

If the Assembly wishes to create or abolish a Legislative Council, it must pass a resolution by a special majority.

This special majority consists of a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

Once such a resolution is passed, the matter is referred to Parliament.

Parliament may then enact a law creating or abolishing the Legislative Council.

An important constitutional feature is that Parliament passes this law by a simple majority, not by a special majority.

Furthermore, such a law is expressly declared not to be a constitutional amendment under Article 368.

As a result, the process is considerably simpler than the procedure required for amending the Constitution.

This flexibility allows states to adapt their legislative structures according to changing political and administrative requirements.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember the distinction between the special majority required in the State Assembly and the simple majority required in Parliament.

StageRequirement
State Legislative Assembly ResolutionSpecial Majority
Parliamentary ApprovalSimple Majority
Constitutional Amendment Required?No
ArticleSubject
Article 169Creation or Abolition of Legislative Councils
Exam Trap: Creation or abolition of a Legislative Council is not a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368. Parliament acts through an ordinary law passed by a simple majority.
UPSC Revision: Under Article 169, a State Assembly must first pass a special majority resolution. Parliament can then create or abolish a Legislative Council through a law passed by a simple majority, without amending the Constitution.

Composition of the Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad)

Statement: The Legislative Council is a permanent house with a unique system of indirect election and nomination representing different sections of society.

The Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) is the upper house in states that have a bicameral legislature.

Unlike the Legislative Assembly, it is not directly elected by the people.

The Constitution prescribes a special composition designed to provide representation to local bodies, educational institutions, and experienced individuals from public life.

The total strength of a Legislative Council cannot exceed one-third of the total strength of the Legislative Assembly.

At the same time, it cannot have fewer than 40 members.

Members are chosen through a combination of indirect elections and nomination.

Approximately one-third of the members are elected by local authorities such as municipalities, district boards, and other local bodies.

Another one-third are elected by the members of the Legislative Assembly from persons who are not members of the Assembly.

One-twelfth are elected by graduates of at least three years' standing residing in the state.

One-twelfth are elected by teachers with at least three years' experience in educational institutions of a specified standard.

The remaining one-sixth are nominated by the Governor.

The Governor's nominees must possess special knowledge or practical experience in fields such as literature, science, art, cooperative movement, or social service.

This mixed composition is intended to bring expertise and diverse perspectives into the legislative process.

For UPSC examinations, the proportional distribution of seats and the categories represented are frequently tested.

CategoryProportion
Elected by Local Bodies1/3
Elected by MLAs1/3
Elected by Graduates1/12
Elected by Teachers1/12
Nominated by Governor1/6
Membership LimitsRule
Maximum Strength1/3 of Legislative Assembly Strength
Minimum Strength40 Members
Exam Trap: The Governor nominates 1/6th of the members of the Legislative Council, not 1/12th. These nominees are chosen for expertise in literature, science, art, cooperative movement, or social service.
UPSC Revision: The Legislative Council's composition is: 1/3 local bodies, 1/3 MLAs, 1/12 graduates, 1/12 teachers, and 1/6 nominated by the Governor. Its strength cannot exceed one-third of the Assembly's strength and cannot be below 40 members.

No Joint Sitting in State Legislatures

Statement: Unlike Parliament, the Constitution does not provide any mechanism for a joint sitting of the Legislative Assembly and Legislative Council to resolve legislative deadlocks.

India's Parliament possesses a constitutional mechanism under Article 108 to resolve deadlocks between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha through a Joint Sitting.

However, the constitutional design for state legislatures is fundamentally different.

Where a state has both a Legislative Assembly and a Legislative Council, the Constitution does not provide any provision for a joint sitting.

This means that disagreements between the two Houses cannot be settled by convening members together in a common session.

The absence of a joint sitting reflects the intentionally weaker constitutional position assigned to the Legislative Council.

The Legislative Council was designed as a revising and delaying chamber rather than a co-equal legislative body.

As a result, the Constitution ultimately gives primacy to the Legislative Assembly.

Whenever a disagreement arises over an ordinary bill, the Legislative Council can only delay the legislative process for a limited period.

It cannot permanently block legislation approved by the Assembly.

This arrangement ensures that the will of the directly elected house ultimately prevails.

The Legislative Assembly therefore occupies a constitutionally superior position in state law-making compared to the Legislative Council.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should clearly distinguish Parliament and State Legislatures on this point.

Parliament has a joint sitting mechanism; State Legislatures do not.

InstitutionJoint Sitting Available?
ParliamentYes (Article 108)
State LegislatureNo
Exam Trap: A common mistake is assuming that states follow the same deadlock-resolution mechanism as Parliament. No constitutional provision exists for a joint sitting of the Legislative Assembly and Legislative Council.
UPSC Revision: State Legislatures have no joint sitting provision. In case of disagreement between the Assembly and the Council, the Assembly's will ultimately prevails through the special procedure laid down by the Constitution.

Legislative Assembly's Power to Override the Legislative Council

Statement: The Legislative Council can delay an ordinary bill but cannot permanently block legislation passed by the Legislative Assembly.

The Constitution grants a stronger legislative position to the Legislative Assembly than to the Legislative Council.

This superiority becomes particularly visible when an ordinary bill is passed by the Assembly and sent to the Council.

If the Legislative Council rejects the bill, fails to pass it, or keeps it pending for more than three months, the Legislative Assembly may pass the bill again.

After being passed for the second time by the Assembly, the bill is again transmitted to the Legislative Council.

At this stage, the Council's delaying power becomes even more limited.

If the Council rejects the bill again, suggests amendments not acceptable to the Assembly, or delays the bill for more than one month, the bill is deemed to have been passed by both Houses in the form approved by the Assembly.

Thus, the Legislative Council cannot permanently veto an ordinary bill.

Its role is restricted to reconsideration, revision, and temporary delay.

The maximum period for which the Council can delay an ordinary bill is therefore four months in total—three months during the first stage and one month during the second stage.

This mechanism ensures that the directly elected Legislative Assembly ultimately prevails in the legislative process.

The constitutional design reflects the democratic principle that the House directly elected by the people should possess the final legislative authority.

For UPSC examinations, the time limits of three months and one month are frequently tested.

StageMaximum Delay by Council
First Consideration3 Months
Second Consideration1 Month
Total Possible Delay4 Months
Legislative Council PowerLegislative Assembly Power
Can Delay a BillCan Ultimately Override Council
Cannot Permanently VetoFinal Legislative Authority
Exam Trap: The Legislative Council has only a suspensive veto. It can delay an ordinary bill for a maximum of four months but cannot permanently block it.
UPSC Revision: If the Legislative Council delays an ordinary bill for more than 3 months initially and more than 1 month after reconsideration, the bill is deemed passed in the form approved by the Legislative Assembly. Maximum delay possible: 4 months.

Advocate General: Constitutional Position and Qualifications

Statement: The Advocate General is the highest law officer of a state and serves as the principal legal adviser to the state government.

The Constitution provides for the office of the Advocate General for the State under Article 165.

He is the highest legal officer of the state government, just as the Attorney General is the highest legal officer of the Union Government.

The Advocate General advises the state government on legal matters and performs duties assigned by the Governor.

The office plays an important role in ensuring that the actions of the state government remain consistent with constitutional and legal requirements.

The Governor appoints the Advocate General.

To be eligible for appointment, a person must possess the qualifications required for appointment as a Judge of a High Court.

These qualifications include citizenship of India and the prescribed legal experience laid down by the Constitution.

Unlike judges, the Advocate General does not enjoy a fixed tenure.

He holds office during the pleasure of the Governor.

This means the Governor may remove the Advocate General at any time, and the Constitution does not prescribe any formal impeachment or removal procedure.

Similarly, no fixed term of office is specified.

The conditions of service and remuneration are determined by the Governor.

For UPSC examinations, the key points to remember are Article 165, appointment by the Governor, qualification as a High Court Judge, and tenure during the Governor's pleasure.

FeatureAdvocate General
Constitutional ArticleArticle 165
Appointed ByGovernor
QualificationQualified to be a High Court Judge
TenureDuring Pleasure of Governor
Fixed TermNo
Exam Trap: The Advocate General need not be a High Court Judge. He only needs to possess the qualifications required for appointment as a High Court Judge.
UPSC Revision: Under Article 165, the Advocate General is appointed by the Governor, must be qualified to become a High Court Judge, and holds office during the pleasure of the Governor without a fixed constitutional tenure.

Rights, Privileges and Legislative Participation of the Advocate General

Statement: The Advocate General enjoys extensive rights of participation in legislative proceedings but does not possess voting rights.

The Advocate General occupies a unique constitutional position within the state governmental framework.

Although he is not a member of the legislature, the Constitution grants him important privileges to enable effective discharge of his duties.

One of his most significant privileges is the right of audience before all courts within the state.

This allows him to represent and defend the interests of the state government in legal proceedings.

The Constitution also grants him special access to the state legislature.

The Advocate General has the right to speak and participate in the proceedings of both Houses of the State Legislature.

In states with bicameral legislatures, he may participate in both the Legislative Assembly and the Legislative Council.

He may also attend and participate in the proceedings of legislative committees of which he is named a member.

These privileges ensure that the government receives authoritative legal advice during legislative deliberations.

However, despite these extensive participatory rights, an important constitutional limitation applies.

The Advocate General does not possess the right to vote in the legislature or in any committee proceedings.

This restriction exists because he is not an elected representative and is not formally a member of the legislature.

Thus, the Constitution balances his advisory role with democratic accountability by permitting participation but denying decision-making through voting.

For UPSC examinations, the distinction between the right to participate and the absence of voting rights is frequently tested.

PrivilegeAdvocate General
Right of Audience in CourtsYes
Can Speak in LegislatureYes
Can Participate in ProceedingsYes
Can Serve on CommitteesYes (where entitled)
Right to VoteNo
Exam Trap: The Advocate General can speak and participate in proceedings of the state legislature, but he cannot vote because he is not a member of the legislature.
UPSC Revision: The Advocate General enjoys the right of audience in all courts within the state and may participate in legislative proceedings and committees, but has no voting rights in the legislature.

The Collegium System and Appointment of Supreme Court Judges

Statement: Judges of the Supreme Court are formally appointed by the President, but the actual recommendation process is governed by the Collegium System.

The Constitution provides that judges of the Supreme Court of India are appointed by the President.

However, over time, judicial interpretations transformed the appointment process and gave rise to the Collegium System.

The Collegium is a judge-made mechanism and is not explicitly mentioned anywhere in the Constitution.

At the Supreme Court level, the Collegium consists of the Chief Justice of India (CJI) and the four senior-most judges of the Supreme Court.

The Collegium identifies suitable candidates and recommends names for appointment as Supreme Court judges.

The recommendations are forwarded to the Central Government, which processes them and advises the President regarding formal appointment.

Although the executive may seek clarification or return a recommendation for reconsideration, it cannot permanently reject a recommendation that is reiterated by the Collegium.

If the Collegium unanimously reiterates its recommendation, the executive is constitutionally expected to accept it.

This system evolved through a series of landmark judicial decisions known as the First, Second, and Third Judges Cases.

The objective of the system is to preserve the independence of the judiciary by minimizing executive dominance in judicial appointments.

As a result, appointments to the higher judiciary in India involve cooperation between the judiciary and the executive, with primacy resting in the Collegium.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that the President formally appoints judges, but the recommendation originates from the Collegium led by the CJI.

FeatureProvision
Formal Appointing AuthorityPresident of India
Recommending BodySupreme Court Collegium
CompositionCJI + 4 Senior-most SC Judges
Mentioned in Constitution?No
Exam Trap: The Collegium System is not a constitutional provision. It evolved through Supreme Court judgments and consists of the CJI plus the four senior-most Supreme Court judges.
UPSC Revision: Supreme Court judges are formally appointed by the President, but recommendations come from the Collegium comprising the CJI and four senior-most Supreme Court judges. The executive cannot override a reiterated Collegium recommendation.

Tenure and Removal of Supreme Court Judges

Statement: Supreme Court judges enjoy strong constitutional security of tenure and can be removed only through a rigorous parliamentary process.

The Constitution guarantees the independence of the judiciary by providing Supreme Court judges with a secure tenure.

A judge of the Supreme Court holds office until attaining the age of 65 years.

This fixed retirement age ensures stability and protects judges from arbitrary executive action.

A judge may resign before retirement by submitting a written resignation to the President of India.

However, removal from office is extremely difficult and is permitted only on specific constitutional grounds.

A Supreme Court judge can be removed only for proved misbehavior or incapacity.

The removal process is commonly referred to as impeachment, although the Constitution uses the expression 'removal'.

The procedure is regulated by the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.

The process begins with a motion in either House of Parliament.

If the required preliminary support is obtained, an inquiry committee investigates the allegations.

Only if the charges are proved can Parliament proceed further.

Both Houses of Parliament must then pass an address to the President in the same session.

The resolution must be passed by a special majority consisting of a majority of the total membership of the House and a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting.

After both Houses pass the address, the President issues the order removing the judge.

This elaborate process prevents political interference and safeguards judicial independence.

FeatureSupreme Court Judge
Retirement Age65 Years
Removal GroundsProved Misbehavior or Incapacity
Removing AuthorityPresident after Parliamentary Address
Majority RequiredSpecial Majority in Both Houses
RequirementCondition
Both Houses Must PassYes
Same Session RequirementYes
Grounds Defined in Constitution?No Detailed Definition
Exam Trap: A Supreme Court judge retires at 65 years, whereas a High Court judge retires at 62 years. Also remember that both Houses must pass the removal motion in the same session.
UPSC Revision: Supreme Court judges hold office until 65 years of age and can be removed only on grounds of proved misbehavior or incapacity through a special-majority resolution passed by both Houses of Parliament and followed by a Presidential order.

Original Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Article 131)

Statement: Article 131 grants the Supreme Court exclusive original jurisdiction over certain federal disputes involving the Union and the States.

The Supreme Court exercises several types of jurisdiction, one of the most important being its Original Jurisdiction.

Original jurisdiction means that a case can be filed directly before the Supreme Court without first approaching any lower court.

Article 131 confers exclusive original jurisdiction on the Supreme Court in matters involving disputes of a federal nature.

These disputes may arise between the Government of India and one or more States.

They may also arise between the Government of India and one or more States on one side and one or more States on the other side.

Further, disputes between two or more States also fall within the ambit of Article 131.

The dispute must involve a question on which the existence or extent of a legal right depends.

The purpose of this provision is to preserve the federal balance and provide a neutral constitutional forum for resolving inter-governmental conflicts.

However, Article 131 does not cover every dispute involving states.

Certain categories of disputes are specifically excluded from this jurisdiction.

Disputes arising out of pre-Constitution treaties, agreements, covenants, engagements, or similar instruments are excluded where the Constitution so provides.

Similarly, inter-state water disputes may be excluded when Parliament provides a separate adjudicatory mechanism under Article 262.

Thus, while Article 131 is broad, it is not unlimited.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember both the eligible parties and the important exclusions.

Dispute TypeCovered Under Article 131?
Centre vs StateYes
Centre & States vs Other StatesYes
State vs StateYes
Private Individual vs StateNo
Major ExclusionsReason
Pre-Constitution Treaties/CovenantsExpress Constitutional Exclusion
Inter-State Water DisputesSeparate Mechanism under Article 262
Exam Trap: Inter-state water disputes are generally not decided under Article 131 when Parliament has provided a separate adjudication mechanism. Also, Article 131 deals only with federal disputes involving governments, not private parties.
UPSC Revision: Article 131 gives the Supreme Court exclusive original jurisdiction in Centre-State and State-State disputes involving legal rights. Pre-Constitution treaties and inter-state water disputes are important exceptions.

Appellate and Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court

Statement: The Supreme Court serves as the highest appellate court in India and also performs an advisory role under Article 143.

The Supreme Court occupies the apex position in India's judicial hierarchy.

One of its principal functions is to act as the highest court of appeal in the country.

Its appellate jurisdiction extends to civil, criminal, constitutional, and other legal matters arising from decisions of High Courts and certain tribunals.

This role ensures uniform interpretation of laws and the Constitution throughout India.

Beyond its appellate function, the Supreme Court also possesses an important advisory jurisdiction under Article 143.

Under this provision, the President of India may refer questions of law or fact that are of public importance to the Supreme Court for its opinion.

The President may also seek the Court's opinion regarding disputes arising from certain pre-Constitution treaties, agreements, or covenants.

After receiving a reference, the Supreme Court examines the issue and may provide its considered opinion.

However, the Court is not always obligated to answer every reference.

In matters falling under Article 143(1), the Court may decline to express an opinion if it considers the question unsuitable or unnecessary for adjudication.

An equally important feature is that the opinion rendered by the Supreme Court under Article 143 is advisory in nature.

The President is not legally bound to act according to the Court's advice.

Nevertheless, such opinions carry enormous persuasive and constitutional authority.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that Article 143 creates an advisory relationship rather than a binding adjudicatory one.

JurisdictionPurpose
Appellate JurisdictionHears Appeals in Civil, Criminal and Constitutional Cases
Advisory JurisdictionProvides Opinion to the President under Article 143
Article 143 FeaturePosition
Reference Made ByPresident of India
Court Bound to Answer?No, in certain cases it may decline
Opinion Binding on President?No
Exam Trap: The Supreme Court's opinion under Article 143 is advisory and not binding on the President. The Court may also refuse to answer certain references made under Article 143(1).
UPSC Revision: The Supreme Court is India's highest appellate court. Under Article 143, the President may seek its opinion on important legal questions, but the Court's advice is non-binding and may even be declined in some cases.

Supreme Court as a Court of Record and Its Power to Do Complete Justice

Statement: The Supreme Court is a Court of Record under Article 129 and possesses extraordinary powers under Article 142 to ensure complete justice.

The Constitution confers several unique powers upon the Supreme Court that distinguish it from ordinary courts.

One such power is its status as a Court of Record under Article 129.

A Court of Record is a court whose acts, proceedings, and judicial decisions are permanently recorded and preserved.

These records possess evidentiary value and cannot ordinarily be questioned when produced before any court.

The status of a Court of Record also carries an important consequence.

The Supreme Court has the inherent authority to punish for contempt of itself.

This power enables the Court to protect its dignity, authority, and the proper administration of justice.

Contempt may be civil or criminal and may arise from actions that obstruct judicial functioning or undermine public confidence in the judiciary.

Another extraordinary constitutional power is contained in Article 142.

This provision authorizes the Supreme Court to pass any decree or order necessary for doing complete justice in any matter pending before it.

The purpose of Article 142 is to ensure that technical limitations or procedural gaps do not prevent the delivery of substantive justice.

Using this power, the Court has occasionally crafted innovative remedies to address exceptional situations where existing laws were inadequate.

However, Article 142 is not regarded as an unlimited power to disregard the Constitution or statutory provisions.

Instead, it operates within the broader constitutional framework while enabling the Court to achieve equitable outcomes.

For UPSC examinations, Articles 129 and 142 are frequently tested because they symbolize the Supreme Court's unique constitutional authority.

ArticleProvision
Article 129Supreme Court as Court of Record
Article 142Power to Do Complete Justice
PowerSignificance
Court of RecordPermanent Records and Contempt Power
Article 142 OrdersEnsures Complete Justice in Pending Cases
Exam Trap: Article 129 gives the Supreme Court the power to punish for contempt of itself. Article 142 is different—it empowers the Court to pass orders necessary for complete justice.
UPSC Revision: Under Article 129, the Supreme Court is a Court of Record with contempt powers. Under Article 142, it may pass any decree or order necessary to ensure complete justice in matters before it.

Common High Courts for Multiple States and Union Territories

Statement: Parliament has the constitutional power to establish a single High Court for two or more States or for States and Union Territories.

The Constitution originally contemplated a High Court for every state.

However, administrative convenience and judicial efficiency often require a broader arrangement.

To address this need, the 7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956 empowered Parliament to establish a common High Court serving more than one state.

This constitutional innovation became necessary after the reorganization of states and the evolving administrative structure of the country.

Under this arrangement, a single High Court may exercise jurisdiction over two or more states.

Parliament may also establish a common High Court for one or more states together with a Union Territory.

The objective is to ensure efficient judicial administration, optimal utilization of judicial resources, and uniform interpretation of laws within the concerned territories.

A common High Court does not reduce the constitutional status or powers of the court.

It continues to exercise all powers and jurisdictions normally vested in a High Court under the Constitution.

One of the most prominent examples is the Punjab and Haryana High Court, which serves both Punjab and Haryana and also exercises jurisdiction over Chandigarh.

The existence of common High Courts demonstrates the flexibility of India's judicial structure while preserving the independence and authority of the higher judiciary.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that the constitutional authority for establishing common High Courts originated from the 7th Amendment Act, 1956.

ProvisionPosition
Can One High Court Serve Multiple States?Yes
Can It Serve States and UTs?Yes
Enabled By7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956
ExampleJurisdiction
Punjab and Haryana High CourtPunjab, Haryana and Chandigarh
Exam Trap: A High Court need not necessarily be confined to a single state. Parliament can establish a common High Court for multiple states or states and Union Territories.
UPSC Revision: The 7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956 empowered Parliament to create common High Courts for two or more states or for states and Union Territories. Punjab and Haryana High Court is a classic example.

Tenure and Removal of High Court Judges

Statement: High Court judges are appointed by the President and enjoy constitutional security of tenure, with removal possible only through Parliament.

The independence of the judiciary extends not only to the Supreme Court but also to the High Courts.

To safeguard judicial independence, the Constitution provides High Court judges with security of tenure and protection from arbitrary removal.

A judge of a High Court holds office until attaining the age of 62 years.

This retirement age differs from that of Supreme Court judges, who retire at the age of 65 years.

Although High Courts function within states, their judges are appointed by the President of India.

The appointment process involves consultation with constitutional authorities, including the Chief Justice of India, the Governor of the concerned state, and the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.

A High Court judge may resign by submitting a written resignation to the President.

However, removal from office is deliberately made difficult to preserve judicial independence.

A High Court judge can be removed only on the grounds of proved misbehavior or incapacity.

The procedure for removal is exactly the same as that applicable to Supreme Court judges.

Both Houses of Parliament must pass an address to the President in the same session by a special majority.

After the address is passed, the President issues the order of removal.

The Governor of a state has absolutely no power to remove a High Court judge.

This arrangement prevents state-level political pressures from influencing judicial functioning.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should clearly remember the distinction between the retirement ages of Supreme Court and High Court judges and the fact that removal is exclusively a parliamentary process.

FeatureHigh Court Judge
Appointed ByPresident of India
Retirement Age62 Years
Removal GroundsProved Misbehavior or Incapacity
Removal AuthorityPresident after Parliamentary Address
AuthorityCan Remove High Court Judge?
PresidentYes (after Parliament's Address)
ParliamentInitiates and Passes Removal Motion

Exam Trap: High Court judges retire at 62 years, not 65 years. Also, State Governors have no role in removing High Court judges.
UPSC Revision: High Court judges are appointed by the President, retire at 62 years, and can be removed only through the same parliamentary procedure applicable to Supreme Court judges. Governors cannot remove them.

Writ Jurisdiction of High Courts (Article 226)

Statement: High Courts possess one of the broadest constitutional powers in India through their writ jurisdiction under Article 226.

Article 226 empowers every High Court to issue writs, orders, or directions for the enforcement of rights and the administration of justice.

This power makes the High Courts important guardians of constitutional governance and individual liberties.

The High Courts can issue the same five writs that are available to the Supreme Court.

These writs are Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo Warranto.

The most important feature of Article 226 is that its scope is broader than Article 32 of the Constitution.

The Supreme Court exercises writ jurisdiction primarily for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.

In contrast, High Courts can issue writs not only for Fundamental Rights but also for the protection of ordinary legal rights.

This wider jurisdiction allows citizens to approach High Courts in a larger variety of legal disputes.

The power under Article 226 is discretionary and equitable in nature.

A High Court may refuse relief if an adequate alternative remedy exists, though exceptions are recognized in cases involving violations of Fundamental Rights, lack of jurisdiction, or principles of natural justice.

Because High Courts are geographically closer to citizens, Article 226 serves as a highly accessible constitutional remedy.

For UPSC examinations, the comparison between Articles 32 and 226 is one of the most frequently tested topics.

WritPurpose
Habeas CorpusRelease from Illegal Detention
MandamusCommand Public Authority to Perform Duty
ProhibitionPrevent Lower Court from Exceeding Jurisdiction
CertiorariQuash Order of Inferior Court/Tribunal
Quo WarrantoChallenge Authority of Public Office Holder
FeatureArticle 32 (SC)Article 226 (HC)
For Fundamental RightsYesYes
For Legal RightsNoYes
ScopeNarrowerBroader
Exam Trap: High Courts have wider writ jurisdiction than the Supreme Court because Article 226 covers both Fundamental Rights and ordinary legal rights, whereas Article 32 is confined to Fundamental Rights.
UPSC Revision: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue five writs for enforcement of both Fundamental Rights and legal rights. This makes High Court writ jurisdiction broader than that of the Supreme Court under Article 32.

Supervisory Jurisdiction of High Courts (Article 227)

Statement: Every High Court exercises supervisory authority over all subordinate courts and tribunals within its territorial jurisdiction.

The Constitution grants High Courts an important administrative and judicial oversight role through Article 227.

This provision empowers every High Court to exercise superintendence over all courts and tribunals functioning within the territories under its jurisdiction.

The objective of this power is to ensure the proper administration of justice and maintain uniform standards in the subordinate judiciary.

Through Article 227, a High Court may call for returns from subordinate courts, examine records, issue procedural directions, and ensure that lower courts act within the limits of their lawful authority.

This supervisory power is broader than ordinary appellate jurisdiction because it is not confined to correcting errors in specific cases.

Instead, it allows the High Court to oversee the overall functioning of the judicial system within the state.

The power extends to both judicial and administrative matters connected with subordinate courts.

However, the Constitution expressly excludes military tribunals from the scope of Article 227.

As a result, High Courts cannot exercise supervisory jurisdiction over military courts or tribunals constituted under military law.

Article 227 plays a crucial role in preserving judicial discipline and preventing subordinate courts from exceeding their jurisdiction.

It also strengthens the independence of the judiciary by placing the lower courts under the supervision of the High Court rather than the executive branch.

Although the power is extensive, courts have consistently emphasized that it should be exercised sparingly and only to ensure justice and legality.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember the distinction between Article 226 (writ jurisdiction) and Article 227 (supervisory jurisdiction).

ArticlePower
Article 226Issue Writs for Fundamental and Legal Rights
Article 227Superintendence over Courts and Tribunals
InstitutionCovered by Article 227?
District CourtsYes
Subordinate Civil and Criminal CourtsYes
Civil TribunalsYes
Military TribunalsNo
Exam Trap: Article 227 applies to all courts and tribunals within the High Court's jurisdiction except military tribunals. Many questions test this specific exception.
UPSC Revision: Article 227 gives every High Court the power of superintendence over all subordinate courts and tribunals within its territory, except military tribunals, ensuring judicial discipline and proper administration of justice.

Appointment of District Judges (Article 233)

Statement: The appointment of District Judges is made by the Governor, but only in consultation with the concerned High Court.

The subordinate judiciary forms the foundation of India's judicial system and is responsible for the administration of justice at the district and local levels.

At the apex of the subordinate judicial hierarchy in a district stands the District Judge.

To maintain judicial independence, the Constitution lays down a special procedure for the appointment of District Judges under Article 233.

According to this provision, the appointment, posting, and promotion of District Judges are made by the Governor of the State.

However, the Governor cannot act independently in this matter.

The Constitution makes it mandatory for the Governor to consult the High Court exercising jurisdiction over the state.

This consultation requirement ensures that judicial expertise plays a decisive role in selecting qualified candidates.

It also protects the subordinate judiciary from excessive executive influence.

A person not already in the service of the Union or State can be appointed as a District Judge only if he or she has been an advocate or pleader for at least seven years and is recommended by the High Court.

The constitutional design reflects the principle that judicial appointments should remain substantially under judicial supervision.

The High Court's involvement serves as an institutional safeguard for maintaining competence, integrity, and independence in the district judiciary.

For UPSC examinations, candidates should remember that the Governor formally appoints District Judges, but consultation with the High Court is constitutionally mandatory.

AspectProvision
Constitutional ArticleArticle 233
Appointing AuthorityGovernor
Mandatory ConsultationHigh Court
Minimum Advocate Experience (Direct Recruitment)7 Years
AuthorityRole
GovernorFormal Appointment, Posting and Promotion
High CourtConsultation and Recommendation
Exam Trap: District Judges are not appointed solely by the Governor. Consultation with the concerned High Court is constitutionally mandatory under Article 233.
UPSC Revision: Article 233 provides that District Judges are appointed, posted and promoted by the Governor in consultation with the High Court. A direct recruit must generally have at least seven years of practice as an advocate or pleader.

High Court Control over Subordinate Judiciary (Article 235)

Statement: Article 235 vests complete administrative control over subordinate courts in the High Court, thereby safeguarding judicial independence.

The Constitution seeks to ensure that the subordinate judiciary functions independently of executive influence.

To achieve this objective, Article 235 places the administrative control of subordinate courts under the authority of the High Court.

This provision is one of the most important safeguards for judicial independence at the district and local levels.

Under Article 235, the High Court exercises control over district courts and all other subordinate courts within its jurisdiction.

The expression 'control' has been interpreted broadly by the judiciary.

It includes matters relating to postings, promotions, transfers, disciplinary proceedings, confirmation of service, and leave of judicial officers belonging to the state judicial service.

By vesting these powers in the High Court, the Constitution minimizes the possibility of political or executive interference in judicial administration.

This arrangement allows judicial officers to perform their duties without fear of pressure from local authorities or state governments.

Although the Governor may formally issue certain orders relating to judicial service, the High Court's recommendations and decisions carry decisive importance.

The constitutional scheme thus establishes a clear distinction between executive administration and judicial administration.

Articles 233 and 235 together create a comprehensive framework for the appointment and control of the subordinate judiciary.

Article 233 governs appointments of District Judges, while Article 235 ensures continuing supervision and control by the High Court.

For UPSC examinations, Article 235 is frequently tested as a key constitutional safeguard for judicial independence.

ArticleSubject
Article 233Appointment of District Judges
Article 235Control over Subordinate Courts
Administrative MatterControlled by High Court?
PostingsYes
PromotionsYes
TransfersYes
Leave and DisciplineYes
Exam Trap: Administrative control over subordinate courts is vested in the High Court, not the Governor or the State Government. This includes postings, promotions and disciplinary matters.
UPSC Revision: Article 235 gives the High Court complete administrative control over district and subordinate courts, including postings, promotions, transfers and disciplinary matters, thereby protecting the independence of the lower judiciary.

Public Interest Litigation (PIL) and Relaxation of Locus Standi

Statement: Public Interest Litigation revolutionized access to justice in India by relaxing the traditional rule of locus standi.

One of the most significant developments in the Indian judicial system is the emergence of Public Interest Litigation (PIL).

PIL expanded access to justice by allowing courts to hear matters affecting the public at large, particularly vulnerable and disadvantaged sections of society.

The concept was actively developed in India through the efforts of judges such as Justice P.N. Bhagwati and Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer.

Traditionally, courts followed the doctrine of Locus Standi, which required that only a person directly affected by a legal wrong could approach the court.

This rule often prevented poor, illiterate, marginalized, or socially disadvantaged persons from obtaining judicial remedies.

PIL substantially relaxed this requirement.

Under the PIL mechanism, any public-spirited individual or organization may approach the court on behalf of persons whose rights have been violated but who are unable to seek legal relief themselves.

This innovation transformed the judiciary into a more accessible institution capable of addressing issues of public concern.

PILs have been used in matters relating to environmental protection, bonded labour, prison reforms, child welfare, women's rights, and the protection of Fundamental Rights.

The judiciary has often accepted letters, newspaper reports, and informal communications as the basis for initiating PIL proceedings in exceptional cases.

While PIL has strengthened social justice, courts have also warned against its misuse for publicity, personal gain, or political motives.

As a result, judicial scrutiny is applied to ensure that only genuine public causes receive consideration.

For UPSC examinations, the relaxation of locus standi is the most important conceptual feature associated with Public Interest Litigation.

ConceptMeaning
Locus StandiOnly Aggrieved Person Can Approach Court
Public Interest LitigationPublic-Spirited Citizen May Approach Court for Others
Associated PersonalitiesContribution
Justice P.N. BhagwatiPioneer of PIL Jurisprudence
Justice V.R. Krishna IyerExpanded Access to Justice Through PIL
Exam Trap: PIL relaxes the traditional doctrine of locus standi. A petitioner need not be personally affected if the matter concerns public interest or the rights of disadvantaged groups.
UPSC Revision: Public Interest Litigation, developed by Justices P.N. Bhagwati and V.R. Krishna Iyer, allows public-spirited individuals to approach courts on behalf of others by relaxing the traditional rule of locus standi.

Judicial Activism and Judicial Restraint

Statement: Judicial Activism and Judicial Restraint represent two contrasting approaches to the exercise of judicial power in a constitutional democracy.

The judiciary plays a vital role in interpreting the Constitution and protecting the rule of law.

Over time, debates have emerged regarding the extent to which courts should intervene in governance and public policy.

These debates are commonly framed through the concepts of Judicial Activism and Judicial Restraint.

Judicial Activism refers to an approach in which courts actively interpret constitutional provisions and legal principles to address social, economic, and governance issues.

Under this approach, courts may step in when there are legislative gaps, executive inaction, or violations of constitutional values.

Judicial activism has often been associated with the expansion of Fundamental Rights, environmental protection, prison reforms, and social justice measures.

Supporters argue that activism helps protect citizens when other branches of government fail to act effectively.

On the other hand, Judicial Restraint emphasizes respect for the separation of powers among the legislature, executive, and judiciary.

According to this philosophy, courts should avoid interfering in policy matters unless there is a clear constitutional or legal violation.

Proponents of judicial restraint argue that elected representatives are better positioned to make policy decisions because they are democratically accountable to the people.

The doctrine seeks to prevent courts from assuming functions that properly belong to the legislature or executive.

In practice, constitutional courts often balance elements of both approaches depending on the circumstances of each case.

For UPSC examinations, it is important to understand that judicial activism promotes proactive intervention, whereas judicial restraint favors limited judicial interference.

AspectJudicial ActivismJudicial Restraint
ApproachProactiveRestrained
Role of CourtsIntervene to Protect Rights and Public InterestIntervene Only When Necessary
View on Policy MattersMay Fill Governance GapsLeave Policy to Elected Branches
Key PrincipleFocus
Judicial ActivismProtection of Rights and Social Justice
Judicial RestraintRespect for Separation of Powers
Exam Trap: Judicial Activism and Judicial Restraint are opposites. Activism encourages proactive judicial intervention, while restraint emphasizes minimal interference and respect for the roles of the legislature and executive.
UPSC Revision: Judicial Activism involves active judicial intervention to enforce constitutional values and social justice, whereas Judicial Restraint advocates limited intervention and greater respect for the separation of powers.

Territorial Jurisdiction & The Doctrine of Territorial Nexus

Statement: Article 245 defines the territorial boundaries of legislative powers enacted by Parliament and State Legislatures.

Indian federalism is characterized by a precise division of territorial sovereignty to ensure both national integration and regional autonomy.

Under Article 245(1), Parliament is empowered to make laws for the entirety or any part of the territory of India, while a State Legislature is restricted to making laws solely for its respective state territory.

Crucially, Article 245(2) declares that no law made by Parliament shall be deemed invalid on the ground that it would have extra-territorial operation. This gives the Union the exclusive power to enact laws governing Indian citizens, property, and corporate entities located anywhere across the globe.

Conversely, laws passed by a State Legislature cannot operate outside its geographic boundaries unless there is a sufficient territorial nexus between the state and the subject matter of the legislation.

The judiciary invokes the Doctrine of Territorial Nexus to determine the validity of such laws, establishing that the connection between the object and the legislating state must be real, substantial, and not illusory.

For the civil services examination, mastering the constraints of territorial limits is essential to understanding the asymmetric aspects of Indian legislative federalism.

Legislative BodyTerritorial ExtentExtra-Territorial Reach
ParliamentWhole or any part of IndiaAbsolute and constitutionally protected
State LegislatureStrictly within state boundariesAllowed only if a clear territorial nexus is proved
Exam Trap: State laws with extra-territorial effects are not automatically void; they are perfectly valid as long as a legitimate socio-economic or functional nexus is established.
UPSC Revision: Parliament holds an absolute monopoly over extra-territorial legislation, whereas states are strictly tethered to their borders unless saved by the Doctrine of Territorial Nexus.

Seventh Schedule Lists & The Doctrine of Federal Supremacy

Statement: Article 246 distributes legislative subject matter via the three lists of the Seventh Schedule, maintaining a unitary tilt.

The operational core of distribution of powers in India is governed by Article 246, which categorizes legislative competencies into three separate tracks within the Seventh Schedule.

List I (Union List) comprises subjects of national importance like defense, atomic energy, and foreign affairs, over which Parliament exercises absolute, exclusive control. List II (State List) details regional items like public order, police, and sanitation, reserved under normal conditions for state legislatures.

List III (Concurrent List) serves as a shared arena containing subjects like education, forests, and criminal law, where both tiers of government retain the right to legislate simultaneously.

To prevent gridlocks, the non-obstante clauses of Article 246 firmly establish the Doctrine of Federal Supremacy. If an irreconcilable conflict or overlapping occurs between a Central law and a State law on a Concurrent subject, the Central law automatically prevails.

This hierarchy ensures that while regional diversities are accommodated, the macro-structural integrity of the Indian legal framework remains uniform and unbroken.

A precise understanding of these lists and the mechanisms of statutory reconciliation is vital for answering multi-layered questions on federal tensions.

Seventh Schedule ListConstitutional AuthorityConflict Resolution Key
List I (Union)Exclusive to ParliamentOverrides List II and List III in case of overlaps
List II (State)Exclusive to States (Normal times)Subordinate to both List I and List III entries
List III (Concurrent)Joint (Centre & States)Central law prevails under Federal Supremacy (Art. 254)
Exam Trap: A state law on a Concurrent subject can override a central law within that state only if it has been reserved for and received the explicit assent of the President of India (Article 254(2)).
UPSC Revision: The three-tier distribution under Article 246 is heavily anchored by the Doctrine of Federal Supremacy, ensuring structural unity during legislative deadlocks.

Article 248: Absolute Residuary Monopolies

Statement: Article 248 vests all legislative and fiscal powers over unlisted and futuristic subjects exclusively in the Union Parliament.

As society evolves, new administrative and technological dimensions emerge that could not have been anticipated by the framers of the Constitution.

To insulate the polity against legal vacuums, Article 248 explicitly vests all residuary powers of legislation exclusively in the Union Parliament. This means that if any matter is not specifically enumerated in the State List or Concurrent List, the power to formulate laws belongs solely to the Center.

This monopoly explicitly covers the authority to impose residuary taxes, a provision legally reinforced by Entry 97 of the Union List.

By structuring residuary allocations this way, India consciously departed from the American and Australian federal systems, where unallocated powers are reserved for the component States to guard regional sovereignty.

Instead, the Indian framework closely mirrors the Canadian federal model, which centralizes unlisted powers to construct a robust, centripetal national apparatus capable of swift regulatory action over modern frontiers like cyber law, space exploration, and AI infrastructure.

Understanding this comparative dimension is highly relevant for analytical essays and descriptive questions regarding constitutional design.

Federal ModelResiduary Power AllocationCentralization Intensity
Indian / Canadian ModelVested exclusively in the Central ParliamentHigh (Strengthens national authority)
American / Australian ModelReserved exclusively for the StatesLow (Prioritizes state autonomy)
Exam Trap: The power to levy a residuary tax is not an inherent general executive power; it requires an express legislative enactment by Parliament under Article 248 read with Entry 97 of List I.
UPSC Revision: Article 248 protects India from legal vacuums by prioritizing a strong center, drawing structural inspiration directly from Canadian constitutional philosophy.

Article 249: Legislative Power of Parliament on State List in National Interest

Statement: Rajya Sabha can temporarily transfer a State List subject to Parliament via a special resolution in the national interest.

While the Seventh Schedule creates distinct areas of authority, the Indian Constitution includes flexible provisions that allow the Center to intervene in state subjects during exceptional circumstances without formally amending the Constitution.

Under Article 249, if the Rajya Sabha declares by a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest, Parliament gains the power to make laws with respect to any matter enumerated in the State List.

This mechanism highlights the Rajya Sabha's constitutional role as the institutional custodian of state interests at the federal level.

A resolution passed under Article 249 remains operational for a period not exceeding one year. However, it can be extended or renewed indefinitely for periods of one year at a time through subsequent resolutions.

Laws enacted by Parliament under this provision cease to have effect six months after the resolution has officially expired. This safety valve ensures that the center's legislative encroachment does not permanently dissolve local governance structures.

Resolution AuthorityRequired Voting ThresholdValidity Period of Law
Rajya Sabha OnlySpecial Majority (2/3rd present & voting)1 Year at a time (Ceases 6 months after expiry)
Exam Trap: Article 249 does not strip state legislatures of their concurrent power to legislate on that specific subject; however, in case of any statutory inconsistency, the parliamentary law will prevail.
UPSC Revision: Article 249 requires a Rajya Sabha special resolution to temporarily empower Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in the national interest.

Articles 250 & 252: Emergency Proclamations & State Consent Overrides

Statement: Articles 250 and 252 allow Parliament to absorb state legislative powers during crises or through voluntary state abdication.

The Constitution provides two distinct structural paths that expand the Union's legislative reach during emergencies or through voluntary inter-state consent.

Under Article 250, when a Proclamation of National Emergency (Article 352) is actively operational, Parliament automatically acquires the absolute power to legislate for the whole or any part of India on any matter contained within the State List. These emergency-driven laws lose their validity six months after the emergency proclamation officially ceases to operate.

In contrast, Article 252 operates via a cooperative yet legally binding mechanism. If two or more states pass resolutions in their respective assemblies declaring it desirable for Parliament to regulate a State List matter, Parliament can enact a law for those consenting states.

However, this mechanism contains a significant legal trap: once a law is enacted under Article 252, the consenting states are completely stripped of their legislative competence over that matter. The law can only be amended or repealed by Parliament, meaning states cannot unilaterally reclaim a power they voluntarily surrendered.

Constitutional ProvisionTrigger ConditionAmending / Repealing Authority
Article 250Active National Emergency (Art. 352)Parliament (Temporary; lasts 6 months post-crisis)
Article 252Resolution by 2 or more state assembliesExclusively Parliament (States permanently lose control)
Exam Trap: Under Article 252, other states can adopt the central law later by passing a similar resolution, but no state has the power to alter or amend the text of that parliamentary act on its own.
UPSC Revision: Article 250 centers power during emergencies, while Article 252 permanently transfers specific state competencies to Parliament based on voluntary state resolutions.

Article 253: International Treaties and Conventions Implementation

Statement: Article 253 grants Parliament total authority to pass laws on State subjects to execute international obligations.

In international relations, India operates as a single sovereign entity, requiring the center to implement international commitments without domestic legislative roadblocks.

To achieve this, Article 253 empowers Parliament to make laws for the whole or any part of India to implement any treaty, agreement, or convention signed with other countries, or any decision made at an international conference or association.

This power allows Parliament to override the Seventh Schedule, enabling it to pass laws on subjects explicitly listed in the State List without needing the prior consent or ratification of any state legislature.

Notable legislation passed via this channel includes the Environment (Protection) Act of 1986 (following the Stockholm Conference) and the TRIPS-compliant amendments to the Patents Act.

Article 253 ensures that India's domestic federal structure does not hinder its ability to fulfill international legal and diplomatic obligations.

Constitutional PowerState Consent RequirementKey Legislative Examples
