However, for the State Public Service Commissions (SPSC), the process features an intentional institutional check. SPSC members and chairpersons are appointed directly by the State Governor.

Crucially, the Governor has no constitutional authority to remove them from office. SPSC members can ONLY be removed by the President.

This removal requires the same conditions as UPSC members, including a formal inquiry by the Supreme Court on grounds of misbehavior. This arrangement protects state commissions from localized political interference.

Commission TierAppointing AuthorityExclusive Removal Authority
UPSC MembersPresident of IndiaPresident of India (Following SC inquiry)
SPSC MembersGovernor of the StatePresident of India exclusively (Governor has no removal power)
Exam Trap: While the Governor can suspend an SPSC member pending an inquiry, the final authority to order removal remains with the President.
UPSC Revision: SPSC members are appointed by the Governor but can only be removed by the President following a Supreme Court inquiry.

Joint State Public Service Commission: Statutory Trap

Statement: A Joint State Public Service Commission is a statutory body created by Parliament, not a constitutional body.

The constitutional framework includes a specific structural distinction regarding the legal status of a Joint State Public Service Commission (JSPSC).

When two or more states determine that a shared recruitment mechanism would be beneficial, their respective legislatures can pass resolutions requesting a common commission. In response, Parliament can establish a JSPSC to service those states.

Crucially, while the UPSC and individual SPSCs are constitutional bodies created directly by the text of Article 315, a JSPSC is a statutory body established by an act of Parliament.

Because it is a statutory creation, its administrative parameters differ from standard commissions: its members and chairperson are appointed and removed by the President, and the body presents its annual report to the respective state governors.

Understanding this legal distinction is important for analyzing the types of public service institutions in India.

Commission FrameworkPrimary Legal Identity StatusAppointing & Removing Controller
UPSC / SPSCConstitutional Body (Created directly by Article 315)UPSC: President | SPSC: Governor (Appoints) / President (Removes)
JSPSCStatutory Body (Created via an Act of Parliament)President of India handles both functions
Exam Trap: A JSPSC handles the duties of an SPSC for multiple states, but it remains a statutory creation of Parliament rather than a constitutional body.
UPSC Revision: A JSPSC is a statutory body established by Parliament at the request of states, with its members appointed by the President.

National Commissions for SCs & STs: The Structural Split

Statement: The 89th Amendment split the unified commission into the National Commission for SCs and the National Commission for STs.

Originally, the Constitution provided for a single, unified oversight body tasked with monitoring the legal and constitutional safeguards for both Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.

Recognizing that these communities face distinct socio-economic and cultural challenges, Parliament passed the 89th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2003 to create more targeted oversight mechanisms.

This amendment split the unified body into two distinct constitutional entities. The National Commission for Scheduled Castes was retained under Article 338, while the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes was established under a new Article 338A.

Both commissions feature an identical structural layout, consisting of a Chairperson, a Vice-Chairperson, and three other members appointed by presidential warrant.

By establishing separate institutional tracks, the amendment allowed each commission to focus exclusively on the specific protection and developmental needs of its respective community.

Target Community GroupConstitutional Article Post-SplitPrimary Functional Focus
Scheduled Castes (SCs)Article 338Monitors safeguards against untouchability and socioeconomic discrimination
Scheduled Tribes (STs)Article 338AOversees tribal land rights, forest safeguards, and cultural protections
Exam Trap: While these commissions investigate specific grievances, their reports and recommendations are advisory and must be laid before Parliament along with memoranda explaining any non-acceptance.
UPSC Revision: The 89th Amendment separated the unified oversight body into the National Commission for SCs (Art. 338) and the National Commission for STs (Art. 338A).

National Commission for Backward Classes (Article 338B)

Statement: The 102nd Amendment Act granted full constitutional status to the National Commission for Backward Classes under Article 338B.

The institutional development of welfare commissions continued with the passage of the 102nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 2018, which addressed the status of the Socially and Educationally Backward Classes.

Previously, the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC) operated as a statutory body established by an act of Parliament in 1993 following the Supreme Court's directives in the Mandal case.

The 102nd Amendment elevated the NCBC by inserting Article 338B into the Constitution, granting it full constitutional status alongside the SC and ST commissions.

This change expanded the commission's authority, empowering it to investigate complaints, monitor constitutional safeguards, and advise on socio-economic development policies for backward classes.

With this shift, the NCBC was granted the powers of a civil court when investigating grievances, establishing institutional parity across welfare watchdogs.

Historical PhaseLegal Identity & StatusPrimary Functional Powers
Pre-2018 FrameworkStatutory Body (NCBC Act, 1993)Advised primarily on inclusion and exclusion lists for central quotas
Post-2018 FrameworkConstitutional Body (Article 338B)Civil court powers to investigate grievances and monitor safeguards
Exam Trap: The 102nd Amendment also introduced Article 342A, which clarifies that the President specifies the list of socially and educationally backward classes for central government purposes.
UPSC Revision: The 102nd Amendment granted constitutional status to the NCBC under Article 338B, bringing it into structural alignment with the SC and ST commissions.

Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities (Article 350B)

Statement: Article 350B provides for a Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities, inserted via the 7th Amendment Act of 1956.

The original text of the Constitution did not include a dedicated institutional officer to monitor safeguards for linguistic minority groups.

Following the re-organization of states along linguistic lines, the State Reorganisation Commission recommended establishing an oversight mechanism to protect minority language speakers in reorganized territories.

In response, the 7th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1956 inserted Article 350B into Part XVII of the Constitution, providing for a Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities (commonly known as the Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities).

This officer is appointed directly by the President and is responsible for investigating all matters relating to the safeguards provided for linguistic minorities under the Constitution.

The officer submits periodic reports to the President, which are laid before both Houses of Parliament and forwarded to the respective state governments, supporting administrative coordination on minority rights.

Constitutional PositionEnacting Amendment ActPrimary Reporting Line
Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities7th Amendment Act of 1956 (Article 350B)Submits regular oversight reports directly to the President
Exam Trap: Unlike the welfare commissions, Article 350B does not specify the qualifications, tenure, or formal removal pathway for the Special Officer, leaving these details to executive discretion.
UPSC Revision: Article 350B provides for a presidential Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities, introduced by the 7th Amendment to monitor language-related safeguards.

National Human Rights Commission: Statutory Identity & Limits

Statement: The NHRC is a statutory body established in 1993, with its chairperson chosen from the senior judiciary.

In contrast to constitutional watchdogs, the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) operates as an independent statutory body, established under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993.

The commission serves to complement the judiciary by reviewing allegations of human rights violations, systemic neglect, or abuse of power by public servants.

The NHRC is designed as a multi-member body. Following statutory amendments in 2019, the chairperson must be a retired Chief Justice of India or a retired Judge of the Supreme Court.

The commission's membership includes retired judges from the superior judiciary alongside individuals with practical experience in human rights administration.

While the NHRC possesses the powers of a civil court to summon officials and review documents, its findings are advisory, meaning it lacks the authority to enforce penalties or award binding compensation directly.

Oversight InstitutionPrimary Governing StatuteChairperson Eligibility Criterion
National Human Rights CommissionProtection of Human Rights Act, 1993A retired Chief Justice of India or a retired Judge of the Supreme Court
Exam Trap: The NHRC cannot investigate human rights violations that occurred more than one year before the date of the complaint, establishing a strict statutory time limit on its inquiries.
UPSC Revision: The NHRC is a statutory body created under the 1993 Act, led by a chairperson from the senior judiciary to monitor human rights protections.

The NHRC Selection Committee Crucible

Statement: NHRC members are appointed by the President on the advice of a six-member selection committee.

To support political balance and institutional autonomy, the Protection of Human Rights Act outlines a specific appointment process for the NHRC.

The chairperson and members are formally appointed by the President, but this action must be based on the recommendations of a six-member selection committee.

This committee includes the Prime Minister serving as the chairperson, the Speaker of the Lok Sabha, the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, the Leaders of the Opposition in both Houses of Parliament, and the Union Home Minister.

By involving representatives from both the executive and the legislative opposition, this selection framework helps protect the appointment process from unilateral executive preference.

Committee LeadershipLegislative Representatives IncludedExecutive Representatives Included
Prime Minister (Chairperson)Speaker of Lok Sabha, Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha, Leaders of Opposition (both Houses)Union Home Minister
Exam Trap: The Chairman of the Rajya Sabha (the Vice-President of India) is not a member of this selection committee; representation is restricted to the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha.
UPSC Revision: The NHRC selection committee balances executive and legislative representation under the leadership of the Prime Minister.

Central Information Commission (CIC): RTI Framework

Statement: The Central Information Commission is a statutory watchdog established under the Right to Information Act of 2005.

The Central Information Commission (CIC) serves to support administrative transparency and institutional accountability within the public sector.

The CIC was established as an independent statutory body under the provisions of the Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005. It acts as the final appellate authority for transparency disputes, reviewing cases where citizens have been denied access to public records by central authorities.

The appointment mechanism relies on a targeted panel: members are appointed by the President based on the advice of a three-member selection committee.

This committee consists of the Prime Minister serving as the chairperson, the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha, and a Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the Prime Minister.

The commission is authorized to review public records and impose financial penalties on non-compliant information officers, supporting the implementation of transparency guidelines.

Oversight BodyStatutory Enactment BaseSelection Committee Composition
Central Information CommissionRight to Information Act, 2005Prime Minister, Leader of Opposition (Lok Sabha), and a nominated Cabinet Minister
Exam Trap: Following statutory amendments in 2019, the tenure and salaries of information commissioners are determined by the Central Government rather than being fixed by the original 2005 text.
UPSC Revision: The CIC is a statutory body established under the 2005 RTI Act, with members appointed based on the recommendations of a three-member selection panel.

Central Vigilance Commission (CVC): Anti-Corruption Watchdog

Statement: The CVC was set up on the recommendations of the Santhanam Committee and granted statutory status in 2003.

The Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) functions as an anti-corruption watchdog, monitoring integrity and transparency across central administrative cadres.

The institutional history of the CVC began in 1964, when it was established via an executive resolution based on recommendations from the Santhanam Committee on Prevention of Corruption.

Initially an executive body, the commission was granted full statutory status in 2003 through the passage of the Central Vigilance Commission Act.

The CVC acts as an independent oversight body, responsible for monitoring vigilance administration across central government ministries, public sector undertakings, and financial institutions.

It is also authorized to review the progress of investigations conducted by the Delhi Special Police Establishment (CBI) into offenses allegedly committed under the Prevention of Corruption Act, supporting administrative integrity.

Evolutionary PhaseInstitutional Framework StatusFoundational Catalyst Committee
1964 GenesisExecutive Resolution BodySanthanam Committee on Prevention of Corruption (1962–1964)
2003 TransitionStatutory Body (CVC Act, 2003)
Exam Trap: The CVC does not investigate corruption complaints directly; it functions as an oversight body that directs inquiries through agencies like the CBI or chief vigilance officers.
UPSC Revision: The CVC is a statutory anti-corruption watchdog created based on the Santhanam Committee's recommendations to oversee central administrative vigilance.

NITI Aayog: The Executive Resolution Blueprint

Statement: NITI Aayog is a non-constitutional, non-statutory body established via a Cabinet Resolution on January 1, 2015.

The institutional approach to national economic planning was modified on January 1, 2015, with the establishment of the NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India).

NITI Aayog is configured as a non-constitutional, non-statutory body. It was not created by a specific article of the Constitution or an act of Parliament, but was established strictly via a Cabinet Resolution.

This administrative action dissolved and replaced the 65-year-old Planning Commission, which had guided India's command economy models since 1950.

Operating as an extra-constitutional body, NITI Aayog functions as an advisory think-tank for the central government, allowing its structure to be updated via executive orders to meet changing policy requirements.

Oversight InstitutionLegal Origin PathCore Structural Status Type
Planning Commission (Historical)Cabinet Resolution (March 1950)Non-constitutional, non-statutory body
NITI Aayog (Current)Cabinet Resolution (January 2015)Non-constitutional, non-statutory body
Exam Trap: Because it was created by an executive resolution, NITI Aayog lacks the statutory powers to issue legally binding directives or regulations independently.
UPSC Revision: NITI Aayog is an extra-constitutional, non-statutory think-tank established via a Cabinet Resolution in January 2015.

Cooperative Federalism & The Bottom-Up Paradigm

Statement: NITI Aayog operates as a strategic think-tank based on cooperative federalism, with no financial allocation powers.

The operational shift from the old Planning Commission to NITI Aayog represents a structural change in how economic planning is managed between the center and the states.

The old Planning Commission utilized a centralized, top-down approach, formulating mandatory Five-Year Plans and exercising the authority to allocate financial grants to individual state ministries.

NITI Aayog replaces this model with a bottom-up approach designed to support cooperative federalism. It functions as a strategic policy think-tank, providing technical expertise and policy designs without imposing top-down mandates.

Crucially, NITI Aayog possesses zero financial allocation powers. The authority to distribute financial grants and budgetary allocations is vested in the Ministry of Finance.

This separation ensures that NITI Aayog's activities are focused on long-term policy research and strategic coordination rather than financial administration.

Comparison AxisPlanning Commission ModelNITI Aayog Model
Planning Flow DirectionTop-Down architecture (Central mandates to local sectors)Bottom-Up architecture (Local inputs shape national strategies)
Financial Allocation PowersPossessed powers to distribute central plan grantsNone (Authority is centered in the Ministry of Finance)
Exam Trap: NITI Aayog cannot authorize or distribute financial resources to states; its role is limited to policy analysis and structural evaluations.
UPSC Revision: NITI Aayog uses a bottom-up approach to support cooperative federalism and holds no authority over financial grant allocations.

Governing Council Architecture

Statement: The Governing Council of NITI Aayog includes the Prime Minister and the Chief Ministers of all states.

To ensure that regional priorities are represented in national policy formulation, NITI Aayog features a structured Governing Council as its primary decision-making body.

The Governing Council is chaired exclusively by the Prime Minister. Its membership includes the Chief Ministers of all States, Chief Ministers of Union Territories with legislative assemblies (such as Delhi and Puducherry), and the Lieutenant Governors of non-assembly Union Territories.

This composition provides state governments with a direct voice in central policy planning, establishing an institutional forum for inter-governmental coordination.

The Council is responsible for identifying shared national development priorities, promoting inter-ministerial cooperation, and reviewing policy implementation across regions, supporting a shared approach to economic development.

Council Leadership RoleState-Level Representatives IncludedUnion Territory Representatives Included
Prime Minister (Ex-officio Chairperson)Chief Ministers of all 28 constituent StatesAssembly Chief Ministers and Lieutenant Governors
Exam Trap: Unlike the old National Development Council (NDC), where state representation was supplementary, the Governing Council embeds state Chief Ministers directly into NITI Aayog's primary decision-making structure.
UPSC Revision: The Governing Council, chaired by the Prime Minister and including all state Chief Ministers, serves as NITI Aayog's primary forum for cooperative federalism.
